proposals.title: Trajectories of Mental Disorder Onset, Persistence, and Remission in a Global College Student Cohort: A Multi-Wave Analysis of the World Mental Health International College Student (WMH-ICS) Initiative
proposals.motivation: The transition to college is a high-risk period for first onset and exacerbation of mental disorders, with long-term consequences for academic and life outcomes. While cross-sectional prevalence is well-documented, pre-2026 longitudinal data on the natural course of disorders in this population—particularly across diverse global contexts—is limited. Understanding the timing, predictors, and patterns of disorder persistence versus remission is critical for designing timely, targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, empirically identifiable trajectories of mental disorder course (e.g., chronic, remitting, late-onset) exist within the college student population, and that these trajectories are differentially predicted by pre-college vulnerabilities (e.g., childhood adversities), college-specific stressors, and access to guided self-help or treatment.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal data from the WMH-ICS initiative, following a global cohort of students over 3-4 annual waves. Using latent class growth analysis (LCGA) and growth mixture modeling (GMM), we will model trajectories of core disorders (MDD, GAD, PTSD, AUD). Time-varying effect modeling (TVEM) will assess how predictors (e.g., academic failure, social support, use of smartphone CBT apps) dynamically associate with trajectory membership. Analyses will control for sociodemographics and prior disorder history.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, global map of mental disorder course trajectories in college students, identifying critical windows for intervention. It will quantify the influence of modifiable risk and protective factors on these trajectories, informing the development of staged, personalized prevention programs. Findings will extend Kessler's prior work on comorbidity and prediction models to a dynamic, longitudinal framework.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies, including Kessler's, have examined comorbidity and risk factors, this proposal uniquely applies advanced trajectory modeling to a multi-year, international college cohort to identify *heterogeneous course patterns*, moving beyond average population trends to subgroup-specific pathways of disorder evolution.

proposals.title: Linking Mental Health Symptom Trajectories to Academic Performance in College Students: A Longitudinal Analysis Using Integrated Survey and Administrative Records
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including the World Mental Health International College Student (WMH-ICS) Initiative, has established high rates of mental disorders among students, but the longitudinal impact of subthreshold and clinical symptoms on objective academic outcomes remains poorly understood. Administrative academic records provide a critical, objective measure of functioning but are rarely linked to detailed mental health assessments in large-scale studies, limiting our ability to identify at-risk students and quantify the educational burden of mental health problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific trajectories of mental health symptoms (e.g., persistent subthreshold depression, comorbid anxiety) identified from repeated surveys will be significantly associated with poorer subsequent academic performance (e.g., GPA decline, course withdrawal), even after adjusting for baseline academic preparedness and sociodemographic factors.
proposals.method: We will link longitudinal survey data from a U.S. subsample of the WMH-ICS initiative, which includes the Composite International Diagnostic Interview and symptom scales, with university administrative records on GPA, course load, and withdrawals. Using latent class growth analysis (LCGA) to model symptom trajectories, we will employ multivariable regression models (e.g., generalized estimating equations) to test associations between trajectory class membership and academic outcomes. Sensitivity analyses will examine the role of CBT skill use, measured via apps like in the RESiLIENT trial, as a potential moderator.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, objective evidence of how mental health symptom trajectories impact real-world academic functioning, moving beyond cross-sectional self-report. It will identify specific symptom patterns that confer the greatest academic risk, informing the targeting of scalable interventions, such as smartphone-delivered CBT, to students in need.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked mental disorders to self-reported academic problems, this study innovatively integrates detailed longitudinal symptom data with objective administrative records to model temporal associations and quantify the academic impact of subthreshold and comorbid symptom trajectories, a key gap in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Temporal Sequencing and Shared Risk: A Network Analysis of Obsessive-Compulsive and Impulse Control Disorder Comorbidity in the World Mental Health Surveys
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 epidemiological studies consistently show high comorbidity between Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) and impulse control disorders (e.g., intermittent explosive disorder, pathological gambling), but the nature of this association remains poorly understood. Clarifying whether these disorders co-occur due to shared underlying vulnerabilities, sequential development (e.g., one disorder increasing risk for the other), or diagnostic artifact is critical for refining nosology and developing more targeted, transdiagnostic interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that OCD and specific impulse control disorders share a latent internalizing-externalizing vulnerability factor, and that the temporal sequence of onset (with OCD typically preceding impulse control disorders) will be mediated by the development of specific maladaptive coping behaviors.
proposals.method: We will analyze cross-sectional and retrospective age-of-onset data from the WHO World Mental Health Surveys, leveraging pre-2026 statistical methods central to Dr. Kessler's recent work. We will employ a two-stage approach: 1) Latent class analysis to identify subgroups based on OCD and impulse control disorder symptom profiles, and 2) Discrete-time survival analysis with time-varying covariates to model temporal associations and identify shared and unique risk factors (e.g., trauma, childhood adversities). Network analysis will be used to visualize symptom-level bridges between disorders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of the temporal and symptom-level relationships between OCD and impulse control disorders in a global, population-based sample. It will identify specific risk pathways that could be targets for early intervention and inform the ongoing debate on the classification of these conditions in diagnostic systems. Findings will directly contribute to the development of more personalized, comorbidity-aware treatment guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented comorbidity, this study uniquely applies temporal and network analytic methods—successfully used in Dr. Kessler's research on other disorders—to unpack the directional relationships and symptom connections between OCD and impulse control disorders, moving beyond simple odds ratios to model dynamic risk.

proposals.title: Network Structure of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder Comorbidity: A Cross-Disorder Analysis of Symptom-Level Bridges to Impulse Control and Mood Disorders
proposals.motivation: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) exhibits high comorbidity with impulse control (e.g., skin-picking, hoarding) and mood disorders, complicating diagnosis and treatment. Traditional latent variable models treat these as distinct entities, potentially obscuring the specific symptom-level pathways that drive co-occurrence. A network approach, which conceptualizes disorders as systems of interacting symptoms, can elucidate these direct connections, offering a more nuanced model for understanding clinical complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific OCD symptoms (e.g., intrusive thoughts, checking rituals) will function as bridge symptoms, exhibiting strong direct connections to symptoms of impulse control and mood disorders (e.g., irritability, anhedonia, impulsivity), and that these bridges will differ significantly between clinical and subclinical populations.
proposals.method: We will apply psychometric network analysis (Gaussian Graphical Model) to cross-sectional and longitudinal data from the World Mental Health Surveys and other pre-2026 epidemiological studies. Networks will be estimated using the `qgraph` package in R with graphical LASSO regularization and the Extended Bayesian Information Criterion for sparsity. Bridge centrality indices (bridge strength, bridge expected influence) will be calculated using the `networktools` package to identify key connector symptoms. Temporal networks will be explored using Vector Autoregression on available longitudinal panels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of symptom-level connections between OCD and its frequent comorbid conditions, moving beyond diagnostic co-occurrence rates. It will identify high-impact bridge symptoms that may be prime targets for transdiagnostic intervention. The findings will inform the development of more precise, mechanism-guided treatment strategies for complex, comorbid presentations.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied within disorders like depression, its systematic use to model the specific symptom bridges *between* OCD, impulse control, and mood disorders is novel. This approach moves past comorbidity as a statistical association to model it as a dynamic system of interacting symptoms, aligning with the NIMH Research Domain Criteria (RDoC) framework for studying mechanisms across traditional boundaries.

proposals.title: Computational Phenotyping of Prodromal Features for Early Detection of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Machine Learning Analysis of the World Mental Health Surveys
proposals.motivation: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) often has a protracted, undiagnosed prodromal phase, leading to significant functional impairment before treatment. Early detection is critical for timely intervention, but current diagnostic tools are not optimized for identifying subclinical, high-risk states. Leveraging large-scale, pre-existing epidemiological survey data offers a powerful, low-cost opportunity to model the emergence of OCD.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model trained on detailed, pre-2026 World Mental Health survey data can identify a distinct computational phenotype—a specific combination of subthreshold symptoms, comorbid features, and impairment indicators—that accurately predicts the subsequent onset of full-threshold OCD.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the WHO World Mental Health Surveys, a preeminent pre-2026 dataset where Dr. Kessler has deep expertise. Using baseline assessments of anxiety, mood, impulse-control, and specific obsessive-compulsive symptoms, we will engineer features capturing severity, persistence, and comorbidity patterns. We will train and validate ensemble models (e.g., Random Forests, Gradient Boosting Machines) and regularized regression (LASSO) to predict OCD onset at follow-up waves, prioritizing model explainability (e.g., SHAP values) to identify key prodromal drivers.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a validated, explainable predictive algorithm for OCD risk based on prodromal features, directly translatable to screening in primary care and college mental health settings. It will advance the field of computational psychiatry by defining a data-driven OCD risk phenotype and demonstrate the utility of repurposing large-scale survey data for disorder-specific early detection.
proposals.novelty: While computational phenotyping has been applied to psychosis and depression, its focused application to modeling the OCD prodrome using comprehensive, international survey data is novel. The approach integrates Dr. Kessler's expertise in comorbidity and risk modeling with emerging, pre-2026 machine learning methods for explainable prediction in mental health.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Factors Against Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder Onset: A Resilience Study Within High-Risk Cohorts of the World Mental Health Surveys
proposals.motivation: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) exhibits high lifetime comorbidity with other mental disorders, suggesting shared etiological pathways. However, most individuals with these risk factors do not develop OCD, a phenomenon of resilience that is poorly understood. Identifying modifiable protective factors is crucial for developing targeted prevention strategies, moving beyond a purely deficit-focused model of psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific psychological, social, and cognitive-behavioral factors (e.g., high cognitive flexibility, strong social support, specific coping styles) will significantly differentiate individuals at high clinical risk who do not develop OCD from those who do, independent of demographic and comorbid disorder status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal data from the WHO World Mental Health Surveys, focusing on respondents with high risk (e.g., subthreshold OCD symptoms, family history, or comorbid anxiety/depression) who did not develop full OCD. Using machine learning, we will train and compare penalized regression (LASSO) and random forest models to identify protective factors predictive of resilience. Models will be validated in held-out samples, controlling for baseline symptom severity and comorbidity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-based, multivariate profile of resilience against OCD onset, identifying potentially modifiable protective targets. It will shift the research paradigm from pure risk prediction to a balanced risk-resilience framework, directly informing the development of indicated prevention programs for at-risk individuals.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled risk for OCD onset, systematically studying the "inverse"—resilience in high-risk individuals—using large-scale epidemiological data and modern prediction methods is novel. It applies a resilience framework common in trauma research to the specific context of OCD etiology.

proposals.title: Long-term Durability and Preventive Effects of a Smartphone-Delivered CBT Intervention for Subthreshold Depression: A 3-Year Follow-up of the RESiLIENT Trial Cohort
proposals.motivation: Subthreshold depression is a major risk factor for the onset of major depressive episodes (MDEs), yet scalable preventive interventions are lacking. While digital mental health tools like smartphone apps show promise in short-term trials, their long-term effectiveness in preventing clinical depression and sustaining symptom reduction remains unknown. This gap limits clinical and public health guidance on using these tools for primary prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that participants who received the smartphone-based cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) intervention in the RESiLIENT trial will show a significantly lower incidence of major depressive episodes over a 3-year follow-up period compared to the control group, and that intervention gains in depressive symptoms will be durable.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year longitudinal follow-up assessment of the original RESiLIENT RCT cohort (N=~1000). Using annual structured diagnostic interviews (CIDI-SCID) and symptom scales (PHQ-9), we will model time-to-MDE onset using Cox proportional hazards regression. We will assess symptom trajectories using latent growth curve models. Machine learning techniques (e.g., random survival forests) will be applied to pre-2026 data (baseline demographics, clinical features, app engagement metrics) to identify subgroups with differential long-term outcomes and risk profiles for recurrence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term evidence on the preventive potential of a scalable digital intervention for subthreshold depression. It will inform public health strategies by quantifying the intervention's impact on the incidence of clinical depression over time. The findings will also identify predictors of sustained benefit, guiding personalized implementation.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond short-term efficacy to directly test the core public health promise of digital interventions: long-term prevention of major depressive episodes. The application of predictive risk modeling to long-term outcomes of a preventive digital trial is a novel integration of Kessler's expertise in psychiatric epidemiology and risk prediction.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Incremental Validity of a General Resilience Factor: A Comparison with Symptom Absence in Predicting Post-Trauma Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Recent psychometric work proposes a general 'r factor' of resilience, distinct from the absence of psychopathology. However, in clinical and public health contexts, resilience is often pragmatically defined as maintaining low symptom levels despite adversity. It is critical to test whether a new latent factor offers predictive or explanatory utility beyond established, simpler symptom-based definitions, especially for informing scalable interventions and risk models in populations like military personnel and students.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a latent resilience factor (r factor) will not demonstrate significant incremental validity over a simple, observed measure of symptom absence (e.g., low depression/anxiety/PTSD scores) in predicting stable mental health following significant life stressors or trauma exposure.
proposals.method: We will use data from the Army STARRS-LS and the World Mental Health International College Student Initiative. Using structural equation modeling, we will first model the r factor using proposed indicators (e.g., perceived stress, optimism, coping). We will then conduct hierarchical regression and ROC curve analyses to compare its predictive power against a binary or continuous 'symptom absence' variable (derived from standard scales like the PHQ-9, GAD-7, PCL-5) for outcomes including incident disorders and suicide-related behaviors over 12-24 month follow-ups. Machine learning models (e.g., random forests) will be used as a robustness check to assess variable importance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide an empirical test of the necessity of a novel resilience construct for etiological and predictive research. It will clarify whether resource-intensive measurement of a latent resilience factor is justified, or if symptom monitoring alone is sufficient for identifying resilient trajectories in high-risk populations, directly informing the design of future risk assessment tools and preventive interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has developed resilience factors, few studies have directly and rigorously tested their incremental predictive validity against the straightforward operationalization of resilience as the absence of pathology following adversity, a critical gap for translating resilience research into practical clinical and public health tools.

proposals.title: Beyond Symptom Absence: A Multi-Dimensional Latent Factor Analysis of Resilience as Well-Being in Trauma-Exposed Populations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 resilience research, including work by Kessler and colleagues, often defines resilience as the absence of psychopathology or maintenance of social/role functioning following adversity. However, positive psychology and well-being science suggest that thriving, not just surviving, is a critical outcome. It remains unclear whether resilience factors (r-factors) derived from well-being indicators (e.g., life satisfaction, purpose, positive affect) are distinct from those derived from traditional functional or diagnostic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a well-being-based resilience factor will constitute a statistically and conceptually distinct latent dimension from resilience factors defined by the absence of mental disorders or the preservation of social/occupational functioning, and that this well-being factor will be a stronger predictor of long-term life satisfaction and health.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal data from the Army STARRS-LS and the World Mental Health International College Student Initiative. Using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and exploratory structural equation modeling (ESEM), we will model separate latent resilience factors at multiple time points post-trauma/exposure: one defined by low disorder symptoms (PHQ-9, PTSD Checklist), one by functional outcomes (WHO Disability Assessment Schedule, role impairment), and one by well-being indicators (Satisfaction with Life Scale, scales of meaning/purpose, positive affect subscales). We will then use cross-lagged panel models to test temporal precedence and predictive validity of these factors for subsequent outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence for whether resilience should be conceptualized and measured as more than the absence of pathology. It will advance the field by proposing a quantifiable, well-being-based r-factor. The findings could directly inform the development of more holistic resilience-building interventions, such as adapted CBT or smartphone apps, that target positive psychological constructs alongside symptom reduction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined correlates of well-being, no study has explicitly tested whether the latent structure of resilience differs when defined by positive well-being versus traditional deficit-based metrics within the same high-risk, longitudinal cohorts used in cutting-edge psychopathology research.

proposals.title: Modeling Multi-Year Trajectories of Dynamic Resilience Factors Following Trauma Exposure: A Longitudinal Analysis of the Army STARRS-LS Dataset
proposals.motivation: While resilience is a dynamic process, most longitudinal studies track outcomes for only 6-12 months post-trauma, missing critical long-term adaptation and relapse patterns. Understanding how resilience factors like coping skills, social support, and cognitive appraisals evolve over years is crucial for identifying optimal intervention timing and preventing delayed-onset disorders, a significant concern in military and civilian populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that resilience factors exhibit distinct multi-year trajectories (e.g., stable high, deteriorating, recovering) following trauma exposure, and that membership in these trajectory classes is predicted by baseline risk factors and predicts differential long-term mental health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Army Study to Assess Risk and Resilience in Servicemembers – Longitudinal Study (STARRS-LS) dataset, with repeated measures over multiple years. Using latent growth mixture modeling (LGMM), we will identify distinct trajectories of a composite resilience factor derived from validated scales of coping self-efficacy, unit support, and psychological well-being. We will then use multinomial logistic regression to model baseline predictors (e.g., pre-trauma psychopathology, childhood adversity) of trajectory class membership and Cox proportional hazards models to examine associations between trajectories and subsequent onset of PTSD, depression, and suicidality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of multi-year resilience trajectories in a high-risk population, moving beyond static or short-term definitions. It will identify modifiable factors that sustain long-term resilience and critical time windows for secondary prevention, directly informing the development of staged, long-term support protocols.
proposals.novelty: This extends Kessler's prior work on short-term prediction models by applying advanced longitudinal mixture modeling to resilience factors themselves over a multi-year timeframe, shifting focus from predicting disorder to modeling the dynamic process of positive adaptation that mitigates disorder.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Smartphone-Delivered Cognitive Training Intervention Targeting Reward and Threat Circuitry for the Prevention of Post-Traumatic Stress Symptoms in High-Risk Service Members
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including the Army STARRS-LS, has identified aberrant neural activity in reward (e.g., ventral striatum) and threat (e.g., amygdala, insula) circuits as key predictors of post-traumatic psychopathology. However, scalable, mechanism-targeted preventive interventions for at-risk populations, such as military personnel, are lacking. This gap represents a critical missed opportunity for early intervention to reduce the substantial burden of disorders like PTSD.
proposals.hypothesis: A smartphone-delivered cognitive training intervention designed to modulate activity in reward and threat circuitry will be more effective than an active control app in reducing the incidence and severity of post-traumatic stress symptoms in service members identified as high-risk via predictive models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm RCT with transitioning service members flagged as high-risk by the Army STARRS-LS suicide attempt prediction model. The intervention group will use a novel smartphone app delivering daily, gamified cognitive tasks: Approach-Avoidance Conflict Training (AACT) to recalibrate reward-threat valuation, and Attention Bias Modification (ABM) to reduce threat vigilance. The active control app will deliver neutral cognitive tasks. Neural mechanisms will be assessed pre- and post-intervention via fMRI during monetary incentive delay and emotional face matching tasks. Symptom outcomes (PTSD Checklist-5) will be tracked longitudinally for 12 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first efficacy test of a scalable, neuroscience-informed preventive intervention for PTSD in a real-world, high-risk military cohort. It will advance the field by directly linking intervention effects to changes in specific, a priori neural targets (reward/threat circuits), moving beyond syndromic outcomes to demonstrate mechanistic efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While Kessler's prior work (e.g., RESiLIENT trial) used apps for subthreshold depression, this proposal uniquely integrates neural circuit targets from large-scale risk research (STARRS) into a preventive intervention protocol, creating a closed loop from biomarker discovery to targeted digital therapeutic implementation.

proposals.title: Biological Substrates of Psychological Resilience: Integrating Polygenic Risk Scores and Inflammatory Markers with the R Factor in a High-Risk Military Cohort
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Kessler's work with the Army STARRS-LS, has established the 'R factor' as a robust, general latent dimension of psychological resilience, predictive of post-trauma mental health outcomes. However, its biological underpinnings remain poorly understood, limiting mechanistic insights and personalized prevention. Integrating established pre-2026 biomarkers—like polygenic risk scores (PRS) for psychiatric disorders and circulating inflammatory markers (e.g., CRP, IL-6)—with the R factor can elucidate shared and distinct biological pathways for resilience versus psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the R factor will show a significant negative association with polygenic risk for internalizing disorders and with elevated levels of pro-inflammatory markers, and that these biological measures will provide incremental predictive validity for longitudinal mental health trajectories beyond psychosocial R factor assessments alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing pre-2026 data from the Army STARRS-LS, which includes longitudinal psychosocial assessments, genetic data, and a subset with banked blood samples for inflammatory assay. The R factor will be modeled using confirmatory bifactor analysis of established resilience scales. We will calculate PRS for major depression, PTSD, and generalized anxiety using pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics (e.g., from PGC). Inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6) will be assayed. We will use structural equation modeling to test associations between the R factor and biological variables, and machine learning models (e.g., regularized regression, random forests) to test the incremental predictive validity of biological data for outcomes like post-separation suicide attempts and disorder onset.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated biopsychosocial model of the R factor, moving resilience research from a purely phenotypic to a multi-omics-informed construct. It will identify specific biological correlates that may serve as targets for intervention or biomarkers for risk stratification. The findings will directly inform the development of more biologically-grounded screening tools and preventive interventions for high-risk populations like military service members.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have linked PRS or inflammation to specific disorders, integrating them with a transdiagnostic resilience factor derived from psychosocial scales is novel. This approach tests whether biological risk and resilience operate on a shared continuum or represent orthogonal systems, a key unresolved question in pre-2026 etiological research.

proposals.title: Refining the Measurement of the Resilience (r) Factor: An Item Response Theory Analysis Using the Army STARRS and World Mental Health Surveys
proposals.motivation: The resilience (r) factor, a latent dimension reflecting an individual's general capacity to withstand psychopathology, has emerged as a crucial construct for understanding mental health heterogeneity and targeting interventions. However, existing measurement approaches, often based on classical test theory, may lack precision across the full spectrum of symptom severity, particularly in high-risk populations like military personnel and college students. This imprecision limits the ability to detect subtle but clinically meaningful changes in resilience over time or in response to guided interventions, such as smartphone-delivered CBT.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying item response theory (IRT) to resilience indicators will yield a more precise, continuous measurement scale of the r factor, with superior discrimination across varying levels of underlying resilience, especially at the lower (more vulnerable) and higher (more resilient) ends of the latent trait distribution.
proposals.method: We will apply IRT models, specifically the graded response model (GRM) for polytomous items, to resilience-relevant items from existing large-scale datasets where Dr. Kessler has leadership roles: the Army Study to Assess Risk and Resilience in Servicemembers (STARRS) and the World Mental Health International College Student (WMH-ICS) initiative. The analysis will calibrate item parameters (discrimination and difficulty/threshold) for indicators of adaptive functioning, low distress, and positive coping identified in prior r factor research. Model fit will be assessed using information criteria and residual analyses. The resulting IRT-based scores will be compared to classical sum scores in their association with longitudinal outcomes like post-separation suicide attempts (STARRS) and disorder onset (WMH-ICS).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a psychometrically advanced, IRT-calibrated tool for measuring the r factor with known precision across its range. It will directly inform the development of more sensitive screening tools for identifying individuals at nuanced risk within military and educational settings. Furthermore, it will establish a methodological foundation for future studies tracking resilience changes in randomized controlled trials of digital and guided interventions.
proposals.novelty: While IRT is standard in psychopathology measurement (e.g., for PTSD), its application to quantify the positive, resilience factor derived from comorbidity patterns is novel. This moves beyond validating the r factor's existence to optimizing its measurement for practical clinical and research use in high-priority populations.

proposals.title: Modeling Intra-Individual Resilience Dynamics in Trauma-Exposed Individuals: An N-of-1 Study Integrating Ecological Momentary Assessment and Resting-State fMRI
proposals.motivation: Existing large-scale epidemiological studies, like the Army STARRS led by Kessler, have identified population-level risk factors for mental disorders post-trauma, but cannot capture the dynamic, within-person processes that constitute resilience. Understanding these real-time, intra-individual fluctuations in affect, cognition, and neural circuitry is critical for moving from risk prediction to personalized, just-in-time intervention. Pre-2026, the proliferation of smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and accessible neuroimaging provides an unprecedented opportunity to study these dynamics at the individual level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within individuals exposed to trauma, dynamic fluctuations in resting-state functional connectivity within prefrontal-limbic circuits will prospectively predict same-day and next-day resilience (operationalized as lower negative affect and higher psychological flexibility) in response to daily stressors, and that these neural predictors will show significant heterogeneity across individuals.
proposals.method: We will employ an intensive longitudinal, N-of-1 design with 30 trauma-exposed adults. Participants will complete a baseline resting-state fMRI scan and then a 60-day EMA protocol via smartphone, assessing daily stressors, affect, and coping. Two additional bi-weekly fMRI scans will be collected. We will use Dynamic Structural Equation Modeling (DSEM) to construct person-specific time-series models linking neural and behavioral data. For neural feature extraction, we will apply pre-2026 graph-theoretic measures (e.g., modularity, efficiency) to resting-state networks implicated in emotion regulation (default mode, salience, central executive).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical maps of intra-individual resilience dynamics, directly linking time-varying neural architecture to real-world psychological outcomes. It will advance the field from between-person risk factors to personalized, mechanistic models of adaptation, directly informing the development of more tailored, neural-circuit-informed digital interventions, such as the CBT apps Kessler has previously evaluated.
proposals.novelty: While N-of-1 designs and EMA are established, their integration with repeated-measures neuroimaging to model within-person neural-behavioral dynamics over time is novel. This moves beyond static brain-behavior correlations to test the brain as a time-varying predictor of resilience processes, addressing a core gap in the trauma and mental health literature.

proposals.title: The General Psychopathology Factor (p) in Population Biobanks: Neural Correlates and Predictive Utility for Mental and Physical Health Trajectories
proposals.motivation: The p-factor, a transdiagnostic dimension of psychopathology, has been established in clinical and epidemiological samples, but its neural substrates and real-world health implications remain poorly understood at a population level. Large-scale biobanks like the UK Biobank, which integrate neuroimaging, genetic, and longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) data, offer an unprecedented opportunity to validate and extend these findings, bridging the gap between psychiatric nosology, neurobiology, and population health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robust p-factor can be derived from mental health questionnaire data in a population biobank, that this factor will show specific associations with structural and functional brain circuitry (e.g., fronto-limbic and default mode networks), and that it will prospectively predict not only incident mental disorders but also adverse physical health outcomes captured in EHRs.
proposals.method: Using data from UK Biobank (pre-2026 data releases), we will first apply confirmatory bifactor modeling to mental health items (e.g., from the touchscreen questionnaire) to derive a p-factor score for each participant. We will then employ mass-univariate and multivariate (e.g., canonical correlation analysis) methods to associate this factor with T1-weighted, diffusion tensor imaging (DTI), and resting-state fMRI metrics. Finally, we will use Cox proportional hazards models to test the p-factor's predictive validity for first-onset mental disorder diagnoses (e.g., depression, anxiety) and related physical conditions (e.g., cardiovascular events) over follow-up in linked EHRs, controlling for known sociodemographic and clinical risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the largest-scale evidence to date on the neural architecture of the p-factor in the general population and establish its utility as a transdiagnostic risk marker for adverse health trajectories. It will directly inform the development of early, mechanism-targeted interventions, such as scalable digital therapeutics (e.g., CBT apps), for individuals at high transdiagnostic risk.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined the p-factor in smaller, often clinical cohorts, this proposal leverages the unique scale and multi-modal data of population biobanks to test its neural correlates and, critically, its predictive power for objective, real-world health outcomes beyond mental disorders alone.

proposals.title: Triangulating Post-Traumatic Stress: A Hybrid Convergent Outcome Study of Self-Report, Clinician Rating, and Digital Behavioral Assays in a Military Cohort
proposals.motivation: Current mental health research, particularly in trauma, often relies on single-method primary outcomes, most commonly self-report scales. This creates validity threats from recall bias, social desirability, and the fundamental gap between subjective experience and observable impairment. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need for more rigorous, multi-method validation of core constructs like PTSD to improve predictive models (e.g., for suicide risk) and to ensure clinical trials measure what is both personally meaningful and externally verifiable.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid convergent primary outcome for PTSD, defined by the agreement between self-reported symptom severity, a blinded clinician-administered interview (CAPS-5), and passive digital behavioral markers (e.g., sleep fragmentation, social isolation via smartphone sensing), will demonstrate superior predictive validity for functional impairment and future service utilization compared to any single-method outcome alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Army STARRS-LS infrastructure to recruit a longitudinal cohort of 1,500 service members with subthreshold or full PTSD. The convergent outcome will be operationalized using a latent variable model where the primary construct is defined by the shared variance across three streams: 1) PCL-5 self-report, 2) blinded CAPS-5 interview, and 3) digital phenotyping via a smartphone app collecting GPS, communication logs, and accelerometry. Statistical validation will involve confirmatory factor analysis to establish convergence and Cox regression to test the hybrid outcome's predictive power against 12-month functional outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a concrete, statistically validated framework for implementing hybrid convergent outcomes in psychopathology research. It will yield a more robust, multi-modal operationalization of PTSD for use in future etiological and intervention studies. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation assessment tools and predictive models that are less susceptible to mono-method bias.
proposals.novelty: While multi-method assessment is not new, mandating the *convergent validity across modalities* as the *definition* of the primary outcome itself, rather than using multiple measures as secondary validators, is a fundamental shift in outcome operationalization. This moves beyond current best practices of simply collecting complementary data.

proposals.title: Disentangling Causal Sustenance: A Multi-Cohort, Staggered Longitudinal Study to Isolate the Long-Term Effects of a Smartphone CBT App from Natural Recovery and Confounding Life Events
proposals.motivation: Digital mental health interventions, like smartphone CBT apps, show promise for subthreshold depression, but their long-term effectiveness remains unclear. Pre-2026 research, including Kessler's own RESiLIENT trial, struggles to distinguish sustained intervention effects from natural recovery processes and confounding external factors like life events or other treatments. This causal ambiguity limits the evidence base for scalable public health tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the observed long-term symptom reduction attributed to a CBT app is explained by natural recovery trajectories and time-varying exposure to positive life events and other supports. The app's unique causal effect will be most pronounced in specific user subgroups with distinct symptom trajectories.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-cohort, staggered longitudinal design with an active control (psychoeducation app), tracking adults with subthreshold depression for 12 months post-baseline. To isolate effects, we will use frequent ecological momentary assessments (EMA) for symptom fluctuations and quarterly surveys to quantify life events and other support engagement. Advanced modeling will include Growth Mixture Modeling (GMM) to identify latent recovery trajectories and Time-Varying Effect Modeling (TVEM) to estimate the dynamic, time-specific effect of app use, controlling for measured time-varying confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a robust, causally-informed estimate of the long-term effectiveness of a digital CBT intervention, moving beyond simple pre-post comparisons. It will identify which user subgroups derive sustained benefit and quantify how external factors mediate or obscure intervention effects, offering critical insights for implementation and personalization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of intensive longitudinal data on potential confounders (life events, other supports) with advanced causal inference-ready models (GMM, TVEM) specifically to partition the variance in long-term outcomes between app effect, natural recovery, and external factors, a methodologically rigorous approach not yet standard in digital health trials.

proposals.title: Deconstructing Digital Interventions: A Component-Level Analysis of Mechanisms Driving Mental Health and Substance Use Outcomes in a Smartphone-Based CBT App
proposals.motivation: Smartphone-delivered interventions, like CBT apps, show efficacy for subthreshold depression and related conditions, but they are typically evaluated as monolithic packages. This 'black box' approach limits scientific understanding and practical optimization. Identifying the specific, active components (e.g., behavioral activation, cognitive restructuring exercises, mindfulness modules) that drive improvements in mental health and substance use outcomes is a critical next step for the field of digital mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, theoretically distinct components of a CBT smartphone app (e.g., cognitive restructuring exercises) will demonstrate unique, measurable pathways of change, mediating improvements in primary outcomes like depressive symptoms and substance use frequency, while other components will show negligible direct effects.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of data from the RESiLIENT randomized controlled trial, applying a multiphase optimization strategy (MOST) framework. Using ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data on app module usage, we will employ time-lagged mixed-effects models and structural equation modeling (SEM) to test mediation. Specific algorithms will include longitudinal path analysis to model temporal sequences where module engagement predicts changes in hypothesized mechanistic variables (e.g., cognitive flexibility, distress tolerance), which in turn predict subsequent symptom outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond efficacy testing to identify the 'active ingredients' within a successful digital CBT intervention. It will provide an empirical model of therapeutic mechanisms of change in a real-world delivery format, informing the development of more efficient, personalized, and potent digital mental health tools. Findings will directly guide the iterative refinement of the RESiLIENT app and similar interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kessler and others has established the efficacy of app-based CBT, this study uniquely applies a dismantling/mechanisms-focused analytic approach to high-resolution usage data from an RCT, moving from 'if it works' to 'how and which parts work,' a non-obvious step given the integrated design of most existing apps.

proposals.title: Passive Digital Phenotyping for Predicting Post-Deployment Mental Health Trajectories in Military Veterans: A Machine Learning Analysis of Smartphone Sensor Data
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal mental health monitoring, especially following trauma exposure, is critical for early intervention but is hampered by reliance on self-report, which is burdensome and prone to recall bias. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Kessler's work with the Army STARRS-LS, demonstrates the need for scalable, objective risk assessment tools. Smartphone sensor data offers a promising, passive method to capture behavioral correlates of mental disorders in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multi-modal machine learning model, trained on passively collected smartphone sensor data (e.g., GPS, accelerometer, usage patterns), can accurately predict the onset and course of posttraumatic stress, depression, and suicidal ideation symptoms over a 12-month post-deployment period, outperforming models based on baseline self-report alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal data from a cohort of transitioning service members, linking their smartphone sensor data (collected via a secure research app) with gold-standard clinical interview data (e.g., CIDI) at baseline, 6, and 12 months. Feature engineering will extract validated digital biomarkers (e.g., circadian movement regularity, location variance, social interaction proxies from communication logs). We will employ an ensemble model architecture, combining a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to model temporal sequences with a gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost) to handle static risk factors, optimizing for explainability using SHAP values.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated framework for using passive sensing to augment traditional mental health surveillance, particularly in high-risk populations. It will identify the most predictive digital behavioral signatures for comorbid PTSD and depression trajectories, directly informing the development of low-burden, just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs).
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked sensor data to cross-sectional symptoms, this proposal uniquely focuses on predicting the dynamic, comorbid temporal sequences of disorders following a known trauma (deployment), integrating sensor data with a rich longitudinal clinical phenotype to model illness course, not just state.

proposals.title: Durability of Digital and Guided Interventions for Subthreshold Depression and Anxiety: A 24-Month Longitudinal Follow-up of the RESiLIENT Trial Cohort
proposals.motivation: Digital mental health interventions, like smartphone-delivered CBT, show promise for scalable treatment of subthreshold conditions, but evidence for their long-term efficacy is scarce. Most trials, including the RESiLIENT RCT, assess outcomes only up to 6 months, leaving a critical gap in understanding whether initial gains are sustained or if delayed relapse occurs. This limits clinical and policy decisions regarding the role of these tools in long-term mental health management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the significant reduction in depressive and anxiety symptoms observed at 6 months in the RESiLIENT trial will be maintained at 24-month follow-up for the intervention group, but that symptom trajectories will diverge based on baseline risk profiles and post-intervention engagement with app skills.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month naturalistic follow-up of the RESiLIENT RCT cohort (N=~1000). Primary outcomes are PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores assessed at 12, 18, and 24 months. We will use latent growth mixture modeling (LGMM) to identify distinct trajectories of symptom change. To predict trajectory membership, we will apply machine learning algorithms (e.g., random forests, gradient boosting) to baseline STARRS-LS-derived risk factors (e.g., trauma history, comorbidity) and longitudinal engagement metrics (app usage logs). Survival analysis will model time to relapse to clinical-level symptoms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term efficacy data for a smartphone CBT app in a community sample with subthreshold depression, directly informing sustainability. It will identify predictors of sustained recovery versus relapse, enabling personalized recommendations. The findings will extend the temporal scope of Kessler's work on risk prediction and guided interventions into the long-term maintenance phase.
proposals.novelty: While short-term efficacy of digital interventions is established, this study uniquely integrates long-term symptom tracking with pre-existing risk architectures (from STARRS) and behavioral engagement data to model durable recovery pathways, moving beyond a simple 'does it work' question to 'for whom does it work long-term and why?'

proposals.title: Stability of the Internalizing-Externalizing-Thought Disorder Latent Structure from Adolescence to Early Adulthood: A Longitudinal Test Using the Army STARRS and WMH-ICS Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Kessler's work, has established a robust three-factor latent structure (internalizing, externalizing, thought disorder) for common mental disorders in adult populations. However, it is unknown whether this structure is a stable feature of psychopathology across developmental stages or if it emerges or reconfigures during the critical transition from adolescence to early adulthood, a period of high risk for disorder onset.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-factor latent structure of psychopathology (internalizing, externalizing, thought disorder) will demonstrate strong measurement invariance (configural, metric, and scalar) across adolescence (ages 13-17), late adolescence/emerging adulthood (ages 18-22), and early adulthood (ages 23-30), indicating structural stability.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis using longitudinal data from the Army STARRS (for developmental span) and cross-sectional cohort data from the World Mental Health International College Student (WMH-ICS) initiative. We will fit confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) models to DSM-5 disorder criteria assessed via the CIDI. Multi-group CFA will be used to test for measurement invariance across the specified age bands. Model fit will be evaluated using standard indices (CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR), and the DIFFTEST procedure in Mplus will be used for nested model comparisons.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous test of the developmental stability of the dominant latent structure of psychopathology. It will clarify whether the same diagnostic constructs are measured equivalently across key developmental periods, which is a prerequisite for valid longitudinal risk prediction and for understanding whether etiological models derived from adults are applicable to adolescents.
proposals.novelty: While the three-factor structure is well-replicated in adults, no study has systematically tested its measurement invariance across the adolescent-to-adult transition using large-scale, epidemiologically-sampled data with comprehensive diagnostic assessment, a gap this proposal directly addresses.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of Mental Disorder Onset Sequences: A Longitudinal Study Using the Army STARRS-LS Framework
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies, including the World Mental Health surveys, have used retrospective age-of-onset reports to identify consistent temporal sequences in the onset of mental disorders (e.g., anxiety disorders preceding mood disorders). These sequences are crucial for etiological models and early intervention targeting. However, retrospective recall is subject to bias, and the validity of these proposed sequences has not been rigorously tested with prospective data, creating a fundamental gap in our understanding of developmental psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal sequences of mental disorder onsets identified in retrospective studies (e.g., phobias → other anxiety disorders → mood disorders → substance use disorders) will be prospectively validated in a longitudinal cohort, but with significant modifications, particularly in the strength and direction of associations involving impulse-control and substance use disorders.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Army Study to Assess Risk and Resilience in Servicemembers – Longitudinal Study (STARRS-LS) cohort, a pre-existing, multi-wave panel dataset with comprehensive diagnostic assessments (CIDI-SCID). Using discrete-time survival analysis with time-varying covariates, we will model the hazard of a new disorder onset as a function of prior disorder history. We will employ multi-state Markov models to estimate transition probabilities between disorder states and use directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) informed by prior retrospective findings to test specific temporal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, prospective test of developmental psychopathology pathways, moving the field from correlation to stronger causal inference regarding disorder sequencing. It will directly inform the design of staged, pre-emptive interventions by identifying the most reliable early-risk disorders. The findings will also serve as a benchmark for correcting retrospective recall bias in future modeling efforts.
proposals.novelty: While retrospective sequences are well-documented, this study prospectively tests their validity within a high-risk, transitioning young adult population (military service members), a group where understanding disorder progression is critical for prevention. The application of multi-state modeling to this specific question of temporal precedence represents a methodological advance over prior cross-lagged panel analyses.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Factors Against Psychiatric Comorbidity: A Machine Learning Analysis of the World Mental Health Surveys and Army STARRS
proposals.motivation: Psychiatric comorbidity is the norm, not the exception, leading to greater disability, treatment complexity, and healthcare costs. While extensive research has identified risk factors for secondary disorders, a critical gap exists in understanding modifiable protective factors that could mitigate this risk. Identifying such factors is essential for developing targeted, preventive interventions within stepped-care and public health models.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific psychosocial, behavioral, and treatment-related factors (e.g., high self-efficacy, strong social support, early engagement with specific psychotherapeutic skills) will significantly buffer the risk of developing a secondary mental disorder following an initial diagnosis, even after controlling for known clinical and demographic risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal data from the World Mental Health Surveys and the Army STARRS Longitudinal Study. Using discrete-time survival analysis, we will model time-to-onset of a secondary DSM-5 disorder after a primary diagnosis. We will then employ machine learning techniques, specifically random survival forests and LASSO regression, to identify and rank the most robust protective factors from a wide array of candidate variables, including coping styles, social networks, service utilization, and prior treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond descriptive comorbidity patterns to identify actionable protective targets. It will provide an empirically-derived hierarchy of modifiable factors for secondary prevention. The findings will directly inform the development of scalable, guided interventions—such as smartphone apps or brief protocols—designed to bolster these protective resources in at-risk individuals.
proposals.novelty: This shifts the analytic focus from pure risk prediction to the identification of buffering effects, explicitly modeling protection within high-risk cohorts. It innovatively applies machine learning survival methods to the specific question of comorbidity progression, leveraging large, pre-existing epidemiological and military datasets to uncover non-obvious protective pathways.

proposals.title: Targeting Latent Psychopathology Factors for Comorbidity Prevention: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Transdiagnostic Smartphone-Delivered Intervention
proposals.motivation: High comorbidity among mental disorders, well-modeled by internalizing, externalizing, and substance use latent factors, drives substantial disability and complicates treatment. Pre-2026 research, including Kessler's work in the World Mental Health surveys, demonstrates these factors are robust predictors of onset and course. However, most preventive interventions target specific disorders, missing an opportunity for broader impact by addressing these shared, underlying vulnerabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: A transdiagnostic, skills-based intervention delivered via smartphone app, explicitly designed to target mechanisms underlying the internalizing and externalizing latent factors, will reduce the incidence of comorbid mental disorder onset over a 12-month follow-up period compared to an active control condition.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm RCT with 800 at-risk young adults identified via the Army STARRS-LS and WMH-ICS screening tools. The intervention arm will use a tailored version of the CBT-based RESiLIENT app framework, augmented with modules targeting emotion dysregulation (internalizing factor) and impulsivity/behavioral activation (externalizing factor). We will employ confirmatory factor analysis to model latent factor scores at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months. Primary analysis will use latent growth curve models to test differential change in factor scores and survival analysis for time to new disorder onset, adjusting for baseline symptoms and risk profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence for preventing mental disorder comorbidity by intervening on latent psychopathology factors. It will advance the field from descriptive modeling of comorbidity to active prevention at a transdiagnostic level. Findings will also inform the scalable delivery of factor-targeted interventions using digital tools, extending Kessler's prior work on app-based CBT for subthreshold symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While latent factor models are widely used epidemiologically, applying them as direct targets for preventive intervention is novel. The study moves beyond using factors merely as predictors to testing them as mechanistic intervention targets, integrating psychometric modeling with experimental prevention science in a real-world digital format.

proposals.title: Cross-National Variation in Comorbidity Structures and Temporal Sequences of Mental Disorders: A World Mental Health Surveys Analysis
proposals.motivation: Comorbidity patterns are central to understanding mental disorder etiology and treatment, yet most evidence comes from Western, high-income countries. Pre-2026, the World Mental Health (WMH) Surveys have documented significant cross-national prevalence differences, but a systematic analysis of how national/cultural contexts shape the underlying structure of comorbidity and the temporal order of disorder onset is lacking. Understanding these contextual modifiers is crucial for developing culturally valid diagnostic frameworks, risk models, and targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national context, operationalized by economic development, cultural values (e.g., individualism-collectivism), and mental health service infrastructure, significantly modifies the observed network structure of mental disorder comorbidities and the strength and direction of temporal associations between disorder pairs.
proposals.method: We will analyze cross-sectional and retrospective age-of-onset data from the WMH Surveys across 20+ countries. Using pre-2026 established methods, we will first estimate country-specific comorbidity networks using Gaussian Graphical Models and compare structures via network comparison tests. Second, we will model temporal sequences using survival analysis (discrete-time person-month models) to estimate country-specific hazard ratios for prior disorders predicting subsequent ones. Finally, multilevel regression models will test if national-level variables (World Bank income group, Hofstede dimensions, WHO service coverage data) explain variance in network edge weights and temporal associations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, empirical map of how national context shapes the architecture of mental disorder co-occurrence. It will identify which comorbidities and temporal pathways are universal versus context-specific, informing the cultural adaptation of diagnostic systems like the ICD and DSM. Findings will guide the development of more precise, context-aware screening tools and preventive interventions targeting locally salient disorder sequences.
proposals.novelty: While prior WMH work has compared disorder prevalence, this study applies state-of-the-art (pre-2026) network and temporal modeling to compare the relational structure of psychopathology across nations. It moves beyond documenting differences to formally testing macro-level contextual moderators of comorbidity, a hypothesis implied but not rigorously tested in existing cross-national epidemiology.

proposals.title: Micro-Temporal Dynamics of Comorbidity: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Symptom Interactions in Daily Life Among Individuals with Co-occurring Depression and Anxiety
proposals.motivation: Prior epidemiological work, including the World Mental Health surveys, has established high rates of comorbidity between mental disorders, but these findings are based on retrospective, long-interval assessments. This limits understanding of the real-time, within-person processes that drive symptom co-occurrence and progression. Intensive longitudinal data is needed to move from documenting static comorbidity to modeling the dynamic, potentially causal, interplay of symptoms in naturalistic contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in individuals with comorbid depression and anxiety, momentary increases in core affective symptoms of one disorder (e.g., anhedonia) will predict subsequent increases in specific symptoms of the other disorder (e.g., worry) over subsequent hours, and that these temporal associations will be moderated by individual-level risk factors identified in prior cohort studies.
proposals.method: We will recruit 250 adults with comorbid Major Depressive Disorder and Generalized Anxiety Disorder. Participants will complete a baseline clinical interview and then a 14-day Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) protocol, with 5 random prompts per day assessing core depression (sadness, anhedonia) and anxiety (worry, nervousness) symptoms, along with context. We will use multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modeling to estimate within-person, lagged symptom-symptom networks. Dynamic Structural Equation Modeling (DSEM) will be used to test moderation by baseline trauma history and resilience factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, within-person map of the temporal pathways linking specific depressive and anxiety symptoms in daily life. It will shift the comorbidity paradigm from a between-person, disorder-level phenomenon to a testable model of within-person symptom dynamics. Findings could inform the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) that target critical symptom transitions.
proposals.novelty: While EMA and network models have been used separately, applying mlVAR/DSEM to model symptom-level temporal dynamics in a rigorously diagnosed comorbid sample bridges Kessler's large-scale epidemiological findings with micro-temporal clinical science, moving beyond correlation to test directional hypotheses about symptom interaction.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Factors for Secondary Disorder Prevention Among College Students with Prior Mental Disorders: A Machine Learning Analysis of the World Mental Health International College Student (WMH-ICS) Initiative
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Kessler's work, robustly demonstrates that a prior mental disorder is a major risk factor for subsequent disorders, creating cascades of comorbidity. However, a significant subgroup with a prior disorder does not develop a secondary disorder, representing a critical but understudied resilience pathway. Identifying the modifiable protective factors within this high-risk group could inform targeted preventive interventions for college students, a population with high mental health burden.
proposals.hypothesis: Among college students with a lifetime history of a primary mental disorder, a distinct profile of psychosocial, behavioral, and cognitive factors (e.g., CBT skills, social support, academic engagement) will differentiate those who remain free of secondary disorders (resilient) from those who develop comorbidity.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the WMH-ICS initiative. The analytical sample will be students with a baseline primary disorder (e.g., major depression, anxiety) and no secondary disorder at baseline. Using machine learning, specifically random forest and gradient boosting models (XGBoost), we will predict the absence of a secondary disorder at follow-up. Predictors will include baseline measures of CBT skills, trauma history, social support, substance use, and academic functioning. Model explainability techniques (SHAP values) will identify the most important protective factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond risk prediction to model resilience within a high-risk clinical population, identifying actionable protective factors. It will directly inform the development of scalable, guided preventive interventions (e.g., smartphone apps) tailored for students with existing disorders. The findings will contribute to a more nuanced, person-centered understanding of comorbidity pathways.
proposals.novelty: While most comorbidity research focuses on risk, this study explicitly models successful adaptation (non-progression) among individuals already carrying significant psychiatric vulnerability. It integrates Kessler's expertise in disorder trajectories, risk modeling, and digital interventions by seeking protective factors that could be delivered via guided platforms.

proposals.title: Relative Contribution of Socio-demographic, Psychological, and Environmental Factors to Mental Disorder Risk: A Comparative Analysis Using the Army STARRS and World Mental Health Surveys
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies consistently identify strong socio-demographic correlates (e.g., education, income, marital status) for mental disorders, but the extent to which these factors retain primacy when directly compared to psychological vulnerabilities, polygenic risk scores, and detailed environmental exposures remains unclear. Resolving this hierarchy is crucial for prioritizing targets in public health interventions and precision prevention strategies, particularly for high-risk populations like military personnel and college students.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while psychological and environmental factors are significant, well-documented socio-demographic correlates will account for the largest share of explainable variance in the onset and persistence of common mental disorders when all are modeled concurrently.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis integrating pre-existing data from the Army STARRS Longitudinal Study and the World Mental Health International College Student Initiative. Using hierarchical logistic regression and machine learning models (specifically gradient boosting machines and random forests with SHAP value analysis), we will quantify the relative importance and variance explained by blocks of predictors: 1) core socio-demographics, 2) psychological factors (baseline symptoms, trauma history), 3) environmental exposures (combat, childhood adversity), and 4) available polygenic risk scores (for subsets with genetic data). Models will predict 12-month incidence of major depression, PTSD, and generalized anxiety disorder.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, empirical ranking of risk factor domains, informing the debate on social determinants versus individual vulnerabilities in psychopathology. It will yield practical guidance for risk stratification models, suggesting whether complex, costly assessments add significant predictive value beyond basic demographic data. The findings will directly inform the design of scalable, guided preventive interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often examines factors in isolation, this proposal directly pits socio-demographic blocks against other major risk domains using advanced comparative modeling in large, diverse, longitudinal cohorts. It leverages Dr. Kessler's existing datasets and expertise in a novel integrative analysis to test a foundational but unresolved epidemiological question.

proposals.title: Estimating the Societal Economic Burden of Untreated Mood and Anxiety Disorders: A Population-Based Analysis Using the World Mental Health Survey Initiative Data
proposals.motivation: Mood and anxiety disorders are highly prevalent and a leading cause of disability worldwide, yet a significant proportion of cases remain untreated. Prior to 2026, while the individual-level costs of severe mental illness are documented, comprehensive, population-level estimates of the economic burden specifically attributable to the untreated segment of these disorders are lacking. Quantifying this burden is critical for informing public health policy and resource allocation to scale up evidence-based interventions, such as guided digital tools and cognitive behavioral therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that untreated mood and anxiety disorders are associated with substantial, quantifiable losses in productivity (absenteeism, presenteeism) and elevated healthcare utilization costs, and that this aggregate economic burden is disproportionately concentrated among specific demographic and clinical subgroups.
proposals.method: We will analyze cross-sectional and longitudinal data from the World Mental Health Survey Initiative (WMH) and linked administrative claims where available. The core analysis will employ a two-stage approach: First, using propensity score matching or inverse probability weighting to create balanced cohorts of individuals with treated vs. untreated disorders (based on CIDI diagnoses and service use assessments). Second, applying generalized linear models (GLMs) with appropriate link functions (e.g., log-link for cost data, negative binomial for absenteeism days) to estimate marginal effects of untreated status on outcomes, controlling for comorbidities and sociodemographics. Sensitivity analyses will use machine learning models (e.g., random forests) to test the robustness of the GLM estimates against non-linear confounder interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, cross-national estimates of the productivity and healthcare cost burden attributable specifically to untreated mood and anxiety disorders. It will identify key sociodemographic and clinical predictors of high economic impact, offering actionable targets for public health intervention. The findings will directly inform cost-effectiveness arguments for expanding access to scalable treatments, including digital therapeutics like smartphone apps for subthreshold conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined costs of disorders generally, this proposal isolates the economic impact of the *untreated* state, leveraging Kessler's expertise in comorbidity and risk modeling within large-scale epidemiological datasets like the WMH and Army STARRS. The integration of machine learning sensitivity analyses with traditional econometric methods to strengthen causal inference in this context is a methodological advance.

proposals.title: Passive Digital Phenotyping for Real-Time Tracking of Mood and Anxiety Symptom Severity: A Validation Study Using Ecological Momentary Assessment
proposals.motivation: Traditional mental health assessment relies on infrequent self-report, missing critical fluctuations in symptom severity that are crucial for understanding disorder course and guiding timely intervention. Digital phenotyping, using smartphone sensor and usage data, offers a novel, passive method for continuous monitoring. Validating these passive signals against established clinical metrics is a critical pre-requisite for their use in scalable, real-time risk detection and personalized treatment, particularly for populations with subthreshold or comorbid conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal digital phenotype, derived from passively collected smartphone data (e.g., GPS, communication patterns, app usage), will be significantly correlated with and predictive of self-reported mood and anxiety symptom severity assessed via ecological momentary assessment (EMA).
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 adults with subthreshold or mild-to-moderate depression and/or anxiety. Participants will install a research app that passively collects GPS, call/log metadata, screen-on events, and accelerometer data for 90 days. Concurrently, they will complete brief EMA surveys on mood and anxiety symptoms three times daily. Using pre-2026 machine learning methods, we will engineer features (e.g., location variance, social rhythm regularity, circadian movement patterns) and train elastic net regression and random forest models to predict EMA-derived severity scores. Models will be validated in a held-out temporal subset.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, passive model for tracking internalizing symptom severity in near real-time, moving beyond binary risk prediction. It will directly inform the development of low-burden monitoring tools for integration into scalable interventions, like smartphone CBT apps, enabling just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs). The findings will extend Dr. Kessler's work on risk prediction and comorbidity by elucidating real-time, transdiagnostic symptom dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links digital footprints to broad diagnostic categories or suicide risk, this study focuses on validating passive signals for continuous, dimensional severity tracking of core transdiagnostic symptoms (mood/anxiety). It uniquely integrates this with Dr. Kessler's expertise in population-level comorbidity and RCTs of digital therapeutics (e.g., RESiLIENT), bridging phenomenological tracking with intervention delivery.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of Symptom Co-occurrence in Post-Separation Service Members: Identifying Central Symptoms and Intervention Targets for Suicide Risk
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including the Army STARRS-LS, demonstrates high rates of mental disorder comorbidity and elevated suicide risk among transitioning service members. Traditional diagnostic models treat disorders as latent categories, which may obscure the dynamic, symptom-level interactions that drive clinical outcomes and complicate risk prediction. A network approach, grounded in pre-2026 psychometric theory, offers a novel framework to model these symptom-to-symptom relationships and identify precise, transdiagnostic intervention points.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a network of co-occurring mental health symptoms in a post-separation military cohort, a subset of central symptoms (e.g., specific facets of anhedonia, agitation, or sleep disturbance) will be strongly connected to suicidal ideation and behavior, and that these central symptoms will be more predictive of future suicide attempts than traditional diagnostic categories.
proposals.method: We will apply Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) and Ising model network analyses to longitudinal symptom-level data from the Army STARRS-LS. Using the R packages `qgraph` and `bootnet`, we will estimate regularized partial correlation networks to identify symptom nodes and edges. We will calculate centrality indices (strength, betweenness) to pinpoint the most influential symptoms. Temporal networks will be constructed using cross-lagged panel models to examine predictive pathways and potential causal interactions over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond comorbidity counts to map the functional architecture of psychopathology in a high-risk population. It will yield empirically derived, symptom-specific targets for preventive interventions, such as tailored modules in smartphone-delivered CBT (e.g., RESiLIENT model). The findings will advance the explainability of risk prediction models by highlighting actionable symptom dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to general psychopathology, its application to the specific, high-stakes transition period of military separation is novel. This study uniquely integrates network methods with a rich longitudinal dataset focused on suicide outcomes, aiming to translate network centrality into concrete, pre-emptive intervention strategies within existing clinical frameworks.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Factors and Resilience Pathways in High-Risk, High-Functioning Adults: A Machine Learning Analysis of the Army STARRS and World Mental Health Surveys
