proposals.motivation: Most mental health research focuses on risk factors for disorder onset, creating a deficit-focused literature. A critical, understudied question is why many individuals with significant socio-demographic and trauma exposure risks (e.g., childhood adversity, combat exposure, economic hardship) do not develop mental disorders. Understanding these resilience pathways is essential for developing effective, strength-based preventive interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct, measurable profile of protective factors—including specific cognitive-behavioral skills, social support structures, and personality traits—will differentiate high-risk individuals who remain disorder-free from those who develop psychopathology, and that these factors will be consistent across military and civilian populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis on two pre-2026 longitudinal datasets: the Army STARRS Longitudinal Study and relevant World Mental Health surveys. Using supervised machine learning (e.g., random forests, gradient boosting), we will train models to classify high-risk individuals (based on trauma/risk exposure) into resilient (no disorder) vs. non-resilient (disorder) groups. Model explainability techniques (SHAP values, permutation importance) will identify the most predictive protective factors. We will test the generalizability of these factors by cross-validating models between military and civilian cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlational risk research to empirically identify modifiable protective factors that buffer against disorder in high-risk contexts. It will provide a data-driven profile of resilience that can directly inform the content of preventive interventions, such as smartphone apps or guided self-help programs, by highlighting specific skills and resources to bolster.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kessler and others has modeled risk, this study uniquely applies explainable machine learning specifically to the 'dual problem' of resilience within high-risk groups, leveraging large-scale epidemiological data to move from identifying *who* is at risk to understanding *why* some at-risk individuals thrive.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of Lifetime Mental Disorder Prevalence: Quantifying Recall Bias in the World Mental Health Surveys
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological estimates of lifetime mental disorder prevalence, foundational to public health policy and etiological research, rely heavily on retrospective recall in cross-sectional surveys. Pre-2026 evidence from longitudinal studies like the NCS-A suggests significant recall failure and forward telescoping, but the magnitude and predictors of this bias across diverse disorders and cultures remain poorly quantified. This gap limits the validity of global burden estimates and models of disorder onset and comorbidity.
proposals.hypothesis: Retrospectively reported lifetime prevalence estimates for major mental disorders will show systematic under-reporting compared to prospectively observed incidence in longitudinal cohorts, with the degree of bias moderated by disorder type (e.g., internalizing vs. externalizing), persistence, age at onset, and intervening trauma.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal data from the Army STARRS-LS and other pre-2026 cohort studies with repeated diagnostic assessments (e.g., CIDI-SCID) as a gold-standard prospective benchmark. For disorders assessed in these cohorts, we will compare the cumulative incidence observed across waves to lifetime prevalence reported at the final wave. Statistical analysis will employ generalized estimating equations to model the probability of retrospective omission, adjusting for demographic, clinical (e.g., severity, comorbidity), and cognitive factors. Machine learning methods, specifically random forest and gradient boosting models, will be used to identify complex, non-linear predictors of recall inaccuracy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, disorder-specific calibration factors for adjusting cross-sectional lifetime prevalence estimates, directly informing the interpretation of World Mental Health Survey data. It will yield a predictive model of recall bias, identifying high-risk subgroups for under-reporting, thereby strengthening the evidence base for mental health service planning and etiological research.
proposals.novelty: While recall bias is a known limitation, this study systematically inverts the assumption of accuracy by using high-quality longitudinal data as the validator, moving beyond simple acknowledgment to quantitative correction. It integrates Kessler's expertise in large-scale surveys (WMH) with advanced longitudinal methods (STARRS) and predictive modeling to address a core methodological challenge.

proposals.title: Lifetime Prevalence, Comorbidity, and Risk Factors for Psychotic and Substance Use Disorders in the US: An Expansion of the National Comorbidity Survey Replication (NCS-R) Framework
proposals.motivation: The National Comorbidity Survey Replication (NCS-R) provided seminal data on the prevalence of common mental disorders, but its diagnostic scope excluded lifetime assessments of psychotic disorders and detailed substance use disorders. This gap limits understanding of the true population burden of severe mental illness and its intersection with substance use, which is critical for public health planning and service allocation. Addressing this is urgent given the rising societal concern over substance use epidemics and the severe disability associated with psychotic disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the lifetime prevalence of psychotic and substance use disorders in the US population is significantly higher than previously estimated in clinical samples, and that these disorders show strong temporal and comorbidity associations with mood, anxiety, and trauma-related disorders captured in the original NCS-R.
proposals.method: We will conduct a new, nationally representative survey using an expanded version of the Composite International Diagnostic Interview (CIDI) to include fully structured assessments for lifetime psychotic disorders (e.g., schizophrenia, schizoaffective) and a detailed substance use disorder module (DSM-5 criteria). The study will replicate the NCS-R's multi-stage area probability sampling. Data analysis will employ discrete-time survival models to estimate lifetime prevalence and age-of-onset distributions, and latent class analysis to identify comorbidity profiles. Risk factor analysis will use logistic regression to model associations with sociodemographic and trauma variables from the NCS-R framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative estimates of lifetime prevalence for psychotic and specific substance use disorders in the US, filling a major gap in psychiatric epidemiology. It will generate crucial data on population-level comorbidity patterns, informing integrated treatment models and the design of early intervention strategies. The findings will serve as a definitive baseline for future surveillance of these severe conditions.
proposals.novelty: While the NCS-R and World Mental Health surveys have covered many disorders, a dedicated US national survey integrating validated lifetime assessments of psychosis with detailed substance use disorders within the established NCS-R methodological framework is novel. This approach allows for direct comparison of risk factors and comorbidity with the rich pre-existing NCS-R data on other mental disorders.

proposals.title: Dynamic Phenotyping of High-Risk Trajectories: An Intensive Longitudinal Study of Subthreshold Depression Using Smartphone-Based Ecological Momentary Assessment and Genetic Risk Scores
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in identifying risk factors for major depression, the dynamic processes leading from subthreshold symptoms to full disorder remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including Kessler's own RESiLIENT trial, highlights the public health burden of subthreshold depression but lacks fine-grained, real-time data on individuals at highest genetic or environmental risk, limiting the development of timely, personalized interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-risk individuals, defined by polygenic risk scores (PRS) for depression and/or childhood trauma exposure, will exhibit distinct, unstable patterns of affect, cognition, and behavior in daily life, and that these dynamic signatures will predict the onset of major depressive episodes more accurately than static baseline assessments.
proposals.method: We will recruit a deeply phenotyped cohort of 120 adults with subthreshold depression and high risk (high PRS and/or high childhood trauma scores). Participants will complete a 60-day Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) protocol via a smartphone app, with 5 random prompts daily assessing mood, stressors, and CBT-relevant cognitions, plus passive sensing of mobility and communication. Time-series analyses will use vector autoregressive (VAR) and group iterative multiple model estimation (GIMME) to model within-person dynamics and temporal networks of symptoms. Machine learning models (e.g., random survival forests) will test if EMA-derived dynamic features improve 12-month depression onset prediction over baseline models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide an unprecedented fine-grained map of the real-time psychological and behavioral processes that precede clinical depression in a high-risk group. It will directly inform the development of more precise, dynamic risk indicators and the optimization of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) for prevention, a core goal of the NIMH RDoC framework.
proposals.novelty: While EMA studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates pre-2026 genetic risk data with intensive longitudinal phenotyping in a carefully selected high-risk subthreshold cohort, applying advanced dynamical systems analytics to model causal pathways in real-world contexts, moving beyond correlation to identify actionable intervention targets.

proposals.title: Testing a Contextual Resource Buffering Model: A Multi-Site Randomized Trial of a Digital CBT App for Student Trauma Symptoms Across Cultural and Institutional Contexts
proposals.motivation: Digital mental health apps show promise but have inconsistent real-world efficacy. Pre-2026 research, including Kessler's work on comorbidity and the RESiLIENT trial, suggests outcomes are moderated by individual risk factors and context. A critical, unanswered question is whether app-based resources function as universal tools or as culturally-contingent buffers, especially for trauma-exposed students in varying university environments. This gap hinders the equitable global scaling of interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The buffering effect of a digital CBT app on trauma-related distress will be moderated by a three-way interaction between individual trauma severity, app engagement, and university-level cultural/institutional support. Specifically, the app will be most effective for students with moderate trauma severity across all contexts, but ineffective for those with severe trauma in low-support contexts and superfluous for those with mild trauma in high-support contexts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a stratified, multi-site randomized controlled trial (RCT) across universities participating in the World Mental Health International College Student (WMH-ICS) initiative, selected for high/low cultural-institutional support (measured by pre-existing surveys and policy audits). Students (N~2000) screening positive for trauma exposure will be randomized to a CBT skills app (like that in the RESiLIENT trial) or assessment-only control. Primary outcomes are depression/anxiety symptoms at 3-month follow-up. Analysis will use multi-level moderated regression models, testing the three-way interaction (trauma severity × app use × site support level) on symptom change, controlling for baseline comorbidity as in Kessler's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a contextual resource buffering model for digital mental health, moving beyond simple efficacy questions. It will yield actionable guidelines for matching intervention design and implementation strategy to specific cultural-institutional settings and subpopulations, directly informing global dissemination efforts led by bodies like the WHO.
proposals.novelty: While buffering models exist in stress literature, applying them to digital interventions with a three-way, cross-level interaction (individual trauma × digital resource × macro-context) is novel. It integrates Kessler's expertise in population-level comorbidity patterns with digital trial methodology to address a core implementation science challenge pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Community-Based Participatory Randomized Trial of Mechanism-Targeted Neighborhood Resource Interventions for Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 epidemiological studies, including the Army STARRS and WMH-ICS, robustly link adverse neighborhood environments to PTSD risk. However, most research is observational, limiting causal inference, and population-level interventions are rarely designed to modify the specific psychosocial mechanisms (e.g., perceived safety, collective efficacy) theorized to mediate this link. This gap impedes the development of scalable, community-driven prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neighborhood resource interventions, co-designed to target specific psychosocial mechanisms, will lead to greater reductions in PTSD symptoms compared to a waitlist control, and that these effects will be statistically mediated by improvements in the targeted mechanisms (perceived safety, social connection, collective efficacy).
proposals.method: Using a community-based participatory research (CBPR) framework, we will partner with residents of 8-12 high-risk urban neighborhoods to identify and design tangible resource improvements (e.g., enhanced lighting, pocket parks, community hubs). Neighborhoods will be randomized to immediate intervention or a waitlist control. We will implement a longitudinal cohort design, recruiting ~50 residents per neighborhood. Assessments at baseline, 6, 12, and 18 months will measure PTSD symptoms (using the PCL-5) and the hypothesized mechanistic variables. Primary analysis will use intent-to-treat, multi-level mixed-effects models to test intervention effects on PTSD trajectories, with formal mediation analysis (e.g., using the product-of-coefficients method with bootstrapping) to test the mechanistic pathway.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rare experimental test of the causal pathway from neighborhood environment to PTSD. It will advance the field from risk factor identification to mechanism-targeted intervention, generating a replicable CBPR model for community mental health promotion. Findings will offer concrete, community-validated strategies for municipal and public health agencies.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its explicit engineering of concrete neighborhood changes to alter specific, pre-identified social-psychological mechanisms, and in its rigorous, longitudinal test of the full intervention-mechanism-outcome pathway using participatory methods, moving beyond correlational studies of neighborhood disadvantage.

proposals.title: Modeling Neural and Clinical State Transitions in Response to Neighborhood Resource Flux: An Ecological Succession Framework
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including the Army STARRS-LS, establishes that environmental exposures are critical for mental health trajectories, yet dynamic models linking time-varying socioeconomic contexts to brain function and psychopathology are lacking. Ecological succession models provide a robust, systems-level framework for predicting community change in response to resource fluctuations, but this approach has not been applied to human neuroscience and psychiatry. This gap limits our ability to forecast how individuals' neural systems and clinical states adapt or maladapt to their changing environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transitions between neural reward reactivity-PTSD symptom states over time can be modeled as a successional process, where the probability of transitioning to a healthier or more pathological state is a function of the direction, rate, and type of change in neighborhood resources.
proposals.method: We will establish a longitudinal cohort with annual assessments, including fMRI scans of reward circuit reactivity (e.g., ventral striatum during monetary incentive delay task) and clinical interviews for PTSD. Contemporaneous geospatial data (e.g., satellite-derived green space, business data on food access, police crime statistics) will quantify neighborhood resource flux. We will adapt ecological state-space and Markov chain models to estimate transition probabilities between defined neural-clinical states (e.g., high reactivity/low symptoms, blunted reactivity/high symptoms) as a function of lagged resource change vectors, controlling for individual-level risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic systems framework for conceptualizing mental health as an adaptive process within a socioeconomic ecosystem. It will yield empirically validated models that can predict individual risk trajectories based on environmental change, moving beyond static correlational findings. This directly informs the development of targeted, 'ecological' interventions, such as resource augmentation guided by predictive transition models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct methodological translation of ecological succession models, used to predict forest or prairie community transitions, to the modeling of human brain-behavior-environment dynamics over time. This represents a significant departure from standard regression-based longitudinal analyses in psychiatric epidemiology.

proposals.title: Adversarial Generalization of Trauma-Related Psychopathology Mechanisms: A Comparative Test of Boundary Conditions Across Trauma Populations
proposals.motivation: Generalizability of mental health findings across trauma populations is a critical but unresolved issue. Pre-2026, studies like the Army STARRS and WMH-ICS show strong associations between trauma exposure and disorders like PTSD and depression, but it remains unclear whether the underlying mechanisms (e.g., cognitive appraisals, fear conditioning) operate similarly across trauma types (e.g., combat, interpersonal violence, disasters) and demographic groups. This limits the development of universally effective interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength and pathway of the relationship between trauma exposure and subsequent psychopathology will be systematically moderated by trauma characteristics (e.g., intentionality, chronicity) and contextual factors (e.g., pre-trauma mental health, post-trauma social support), rather than being uniform across populations.
proposals.method: We will employ a strategic, multi-cohort sampling design, recruiting participants from three distinct trauma-exposed populations: military veterans (via STARRS follow-on), survivors of interpersonal assault from urban health clinics, and individuals exposed to natural disasters. Using pre-registered, competing moderation models within a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework, we will test whether attenuation of the trauma-PTSD/depression link in new groups is best explained by measured cultural factors, trauma chronicity, or access to services. Analyses will incorporate machine learning-based propensity score weighting for strong counterfactual comparisons between groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond descriptive epidemiology to provide an explanatory model of where and why trauma-psychopathology mechanisms generalize. It will identify specific, modifiable factors (e.g., service access) that disrupt pathological pathways, offering actionable targets for tailoring preventive interventions like guided CBT apps (e.g., RESiLIENT) for different trauma contexts.
proposals.novelty: Instead of seeking simple replication, the design proactively tests boundary conditions by pitting pre-specified, mechanistic moderators against each other in an 'adversarial' framework, a novel approach in psychiatric epidemiology for moving from correlation to explanation in generalization.

proposals.title: Testing the Ecological Validity of Trauma Response Models: A Multi-Site, Multi-Stressor Framework for Generalizability
proposals.motivation: Trauma research often struggles with generalizability, as findings from one population (e.g., combat veterans) may not apply to others (e.g., refugees or assault survivors). This limits the utility of evidence-based interventions and risk models in diverse real-world settings. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need to move beyond single-context studies to systematically identify the contextual factors that modify trauma outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The relationships between trauma characteristics, established risk/protective factors (e.g., social support, prior mental disorders), and mental health outcomes (e.g., PTSD, depression) are not universal but are systematically modified by ecological context, including cultural frameworks, socioeconomic resources, and trauma type.
proposals.method: We will implement a coordinated, harmonized longitudinal study across 8-10 global sites, each representing a distinct trauma 'ecosystem' (e.g., U.S. veterans, urban assault survivors, refugee populations). Using a core protocol from the World Mental Health surveys and Army STARRS, we will collect identical measures of trauma exposure, symptoms, cognitive-behavioral skills, and contextual modifiers. Data will be analyzed using multi-group structural equation modeling and cross-context moderation analysis to quantify the influence of specific ecological modifiers on symptom trajectories and intervention (e.g., app-based CBT skills) effectiveness.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a novel, ecologically-informed model of trauma response that explicitly maps the boundary conditions of existing findings. It will provide a methodological framework for future generalizability assessments in mental health and inform the targeted adaptation of interventions (like smartphone CBT) to specific population contexts, enhancing their real-world impact.
proposals.novelty: It directly adapts the 'multi-site, multi-stressor' validation framework from conservation biology to human trauma research, a novel interdisciplinary translation. Unlike standard replication, it proactively treats diverse human populations as distinct ecosystems to systematically test for context-dependency, moving from asking *if* findings generalize to explaining *why* and *when*.

proposals.title: Simulating Recovery Trajectories: An Agent-Based Model of Post-Trauma Outcomes Across Diverse Populations and Neighborhood Resource Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 epidemiological studies, including the Army STARRS and World Mental Health surveys, document stark disparities in post-trauma mental health outcomes across demographic groups and geographic areas. However, these observational studies struggle to disentangle the complex, multi-level mechanisms—such as individual-level risk factors interacting with neighborhood-level resource access and quality—that generate these inequitable recovery pathways. A dynamic, computational approach is needed to formally test hypotheses about these causal mechanisms and identify actionable intervention points.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inequities in post-trauma recovery trajectories are primarily driven not by individual risk profiles alone, but by non-linear interactions between an individual's trauma/demographic characteristics and the specific accessibility, quality, and cultural congruence of resources in their local environment.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a NetLogo or Repast framework. Agents will be parameterized with attributes (e.g., trauma type, PTSD/depression symptom severity, socioeconomic status, stigma beliefs) derived from pre-2026 datasets like STARRS. The environment will be a stylized grid representing neighborhood resource landscapes (clinics, support groups, parks), each with defined properties (cost, wait time, travel distance, cultural competency). Agent decision rules for seeking care and engaging with resources will be grounded in behavioral theories (e.g., Health Belief Model). We will use virtual experiments to test how varying resource distributions and policies alter population-level recovery outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for understanding mental health disparities following trauma, moving beyond descriptive associations. It will generate testable, context-specific predictions about which resource modifications (e.g., improving transportation links vs. training providers) might most effectively improve outcomes for specific sub-populations, offering a strategic tool for public health planning.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs have been used in epidemiology, their application to model the detailed interplay between clinically-defined trauma populations and empirically-grounded neighborhood resource landscapes in mental health recovery is novel. This fusion of Kessler's large-scale psychiatric epidemiology with complex systems science represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Generalizability of Post-Trauma Mental Disorder Risk Prediction Models: A Replication Study Across Diverse Trauma Types and Demographic Groups
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Kessler's work with the Army STARRS-LS, has developed predictive models for adverse mental health outcomes (e.g., suicide attempts) following military separation and combat trauma. However, it remains unclear if these models generalize to civilian populations exposed to different trauma types (e.g., interpersonal violence, accidents, natural disasters) and across more diverse demographic groups (e.g., varying race/ethnicity, socioeconomic status, urban/rural settings). This gap limits the utility of these tools for public health planning and clinical screening in broader community settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the predictive performance of models developed for military trauma populations will significantly degrade when applied to diverse civilian trauma exposures and demographic groups, indicating a need for population-specific model calibration or feature engineering.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis using pre-existing, pre-2026 longitudinal datasets containing trauma exposure and mental health outcome data, such as the National Comorbidity Survey series and other trauma-focused cohort studies. We will explicitly replicate the machine learning methodology (e.g., penalized regression like LASSO, random forests) used in prior military-focused studies (e.g., Army STARRS-LS) to predict PTSD, depression, and suicidality. Model performance (AUC, calibration) will be rigorously tested and compared across subgroups defined by trauma type (interpersonal, accidental, disaster-related) and key demographics (race, ethnicity, income, gender).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the transportability of trauma-focused predictive models, informing the development of more equitable and widely applicable public health tools. It will identify specific trauma contexts and demographic factors where existing models fail, guiding future research toward more generalizable or tailored risk algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While predictive modeling for post-trauma outcomes is established, systematic replication and generalizability testing across the full spectrum of trauma types and under-represented demographic groups, using a direct methodological replication approach, is a critical and understudied next step.

proposals.title: Disentangling Gene-Environment Interplay in PTSD: A Sibling Comparison Study of Neighborhood Context and Polygenic Risk
proposals.motivation: Gene-environment interaction (GxE) studies in PTSD are confounded by gene-environment correlation (rGE), where genetic propensity influences exposure to adverse environments. Pre-2026 research, including work from the Army STARRS, highlights the need to separate these effects to identify true causal moderation. This is critical for developing targeted, context-sensitive interventions and understanding for whom neighborhood disadvantage poses the greatest mental health risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, after controlling for passive and evocative rGE via a within-family sibling design, a significant causal interaction will remain: siblings with higher polygenic risk scores (PRS) for PTSD and related traits will show steeper increases in PTSD symptoms following exposure to lower-resource neighborhoods compared to their lower-PRS siblings in higher-resource areas.
proposals.method: We will recruit 800 sibling pairs (N=1600) from existing longitudinal cohorts (e.g., Add Health, HRS). Using geocoded residential history, we will define a time-varying, objective measure of neighborhood disadvantage (e.g., Area Deprivation Index) during adolescence/early adulthood. PTSD trajectories will be modeled using multilevel growth curve models. The core analysis employs fixed-effects sibling comparison models, where the within-pair difference in PTSD symptom slope is regressed on the within-pair difference in neighborhood exposure, moderated by each sibling's pre-specified PTSD PRS (based on pre-2025 GWAS) and DNA methylation markers (e.g., from blood samples).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the most rigorous tests to date of causal GxE in PTSD etiology by minimizing rGE confounds. It will clarify whether neighborhood context actively moderates genetic vulnerability, informing preventive policy focused on structural interventions. The findings will advance methodological standards for genetically-informed epidemiological research on trauma.
proposals.novelty: While sibling designs exist, their application to test GxE for PTSD using objective, time-varying environmental measures and modern polygenic/epigenetic tools is novel. This moves beyond passive control of shared genetics to actively model the interaction within a family framework, a significant advance over standard population-based GxE tests.

proposals.title: Modeling Gene-Neighborhood Interactions in PTSD Risk and Resilience: An Ecological Niche Modeling Approach Using the Army STARRS-LS Dataset
proposals.motivation: Despite known genetic and environmental contributions to PTSD, research has largely examined these factors additively, failing to capture complex, non-linear interactions that may define distinct risk profiles. Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) offers a powerful, underutilized framework for identifying specific combinations of intrinsic (genetic/epigenetic) and extrinsic (neighborhood) factors that synergistically predict disorder trajectories. Applying this to PTSD could reveal critical thresholds and buffering effects in high-risk populations like military service members.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific combinations of polygenic/epigenetic risk scores and multi-dimensional neighborhood resource levels (e.g., socioeconomic, social cohesion, green space) will define distinct 'niches' that non-linearly predict PTSD onset and chronicity, revealing synergistic risk and resilience pathways not captured by additive models.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) algorithm, a leading ENM method, using data from the Army Study to Assess Risk and Resilience in Servicemembers – Longitudinal Study (STARRS-LS). The 'species presence' data will be cases of incident or chronic PTSD. 'Environmental layers' will be constructed from geocoded neighborhood data (e.g., census, crime, park access) and individual-level polygenic risk scores (PRS) for PTSD and related traits, along with candidate epigenetic markers (e.g., FKBP5 methylation). Model fitting will identify the most predictive non-linear interactions and environmental suitability thresholds for PTSD outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, integrative model of PTSD etiology that moves beyond additive risk to define high-risk and resilient phenotypic 'niches.' It will generate specific, testable hypotheses about gene-environment interplay and identify modifiable neighborhood-level factors that could buffer genetic risk, informing targeted public health interventions.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual and methodological adaptation of Ecological Niche Modeling to psychiatric epidemiology is novel, providing a framework to discover specific, non-linear interaction 'recipes' between genes and environment. This contrasts with standard regression approaches that typically test for additive or simple multiplicative effects.

proposals.title: Identifying Geographic Clusters of Co-Occurring PTSD and Mental Health Resource Deprivation: An Application of Spatial Scan Statistics to Public Mental Health
proposals.motivation: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) prevalence shows significant geographic heterogeneity, yet public health resource allocation often lacks precise, evidence-based targeting. Prior to 2026, ecological studies link area-level disadvantage to poor mental health outcomes, but methods to identify specific, statistically significant clusters where high PTSD risk coincides with critical resource gaps (e.g., provider shortages, low insurance coverage) are underdeveloped. This limits efficient intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial scan statistics, adapted from infectious disease epidemiology, will identify statistically significant geographic clusters where observed rates of probable PTSD exceed expected rates, and that these clusters will be characterized by significant co-occurring deprivation in mental health care resources and socioeconomic indicators.
proposals.method: We will use data from the Army STARRS-LS and linked area-level data (e.g., AHRF, ACS). Cases are individuals screening positive for PTSD. The population at risk is the total sample. We will employ the SaTScan software to perform retrospective purely spatial analyses using the Poisson model. Scanning windows of varying size will detect clusters of high PTSD risk. We will then characterize identified clusters using covariates like mental health provider density, poverty rate, and urbanicity, and conduct a multinomial logistic regression on cluster membership to validate associations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel methodological application for precision public mental health, generating a map of high-priority intervention zones defined by both need and resource deficit. It will advance the field beyond ecological correlation by offering a rigorous, cluster-detection framework for resource targeting, directly informing policy and guided outreach programs.
proposals.novelty: While spatial epidemiology is mature for infectious diseases, its application to detect syndemic clusters of mental disorder and systemic resource deprivation is novel. This moves beyond simply mapping prevalence or poverty to identifying specific, statistically significant areas where need and service gap intersect.

proposals.title: Geospatial Hot Spot Analysis of PTSD Risk and Mental Health Resource Deprivation: A Population-Based Epidemiological Study
proposals.motivation: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) prevalence shows significant geographic heterogeneity, but traditional epidemiological methods often fail to identify precise, actionable spatial clusters of risk. Concurrently, access to evidence-based mental health resources, such as trauma-informed cognitive behavioral therapy, is unevenly distributed. Identifying areas where high PTSD risk coincides with low resource availability is critical for efficient public health intervention and resource allocation, particularly for vulnerable populations like veterans and urban residents exposed to community violence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that statistically significant spatial hot spots of high PTSD symptom prevalence will be identified and that these clusters will show significant geographic overlap with cold spots of mental health service provider density and availability of guided, evidence-based interventions.
proposals.method: We will link geocoded individual-level data on PTSD symptoms from pre-2026 epidemiological studies (e.g., relevant Army STARRS or World Mental Health Initiative cohorts) with area-level data on mental health provider locations and sociodemographic covariates. Using Geographic Information Systems (GIS), we will apply the Getis-Ord Gi* statistic to identify statistically significant (p<0.05) hot and cold spots of PTSD risk and resource deprivation. Secondary analyses will use spatial regression (e.g., geographically weighted regression) to model the association between hotspot membership and individual-level risk factors (e.g., trauma history, comorbidity).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, population-based maps of PTSD risk-resource mismatches, moving beyond regional summaries to block-group or census-tract level precision. It will directly inform spatial targeting of public health campaigns, mobile health (mHealth) app dissemination (e.g., for CBT skills training), and the placement of community-based intervention programs, offering a model for precision prevention.
proposals.novelty: While crime-mapping hot spot techniques are established in criminology, their application to psychiatric epidemiology and mental health service planning is novel. This approach integrates ecological resource data with individual-level clinical data to identify specific geographic targets for intervention, a significant advance over non-spatial risk factor analyses.

proposals.title: Modeling the Dynamic Reinforcement of PTSD Risk: An Agent-Based Simulation of Daily Navigation in Geospatial Resource Deprivation Hot Spots
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies, including the Army STARRS and WMH-ICS initiatives, consistently identify spatial clusters where PTSD risk and mental healthcare resource deprivation co-occur. However, these static, ecological correlations cannot explain how the daily lived experience within these hot spots—the navigation of a high-threat, low-resource landscape—dynamically perpetuates mental health inequities. A mechanistic understanding of this process is critical for moving beyond identification to designing spatially and behaviorally informed interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The interaction between the precise geospatial structure of resource deprivation and trauma-informed navigation behaviors (e.g., path avoidance, truncated trip chains) dynamically reinforces PTSD risk and care disparities, creating a feedback loop that sustains hot spots beyond what static risk factors alone would predict.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a Getis-Ord Gi* hot spot analysis on integrated data (e.g., PTSD prevalence from health systems, provider locations, socioeconomic indicators) to map high-risk, low-resource areas. Using the NetLogo ABM platform, we will create a synthetic population of agents with attributes (e.g., PTSD symptom severity) drawn from STARRS-like distributions. Agents will follow daily activity schedules, with movement governed by a decision architecture incorporating trauma constraints (e.g., increased cost for routes through perceived high-threat zones, probability of abandoning trips to distant care). We will validate the model by comparing simulated spatial-temporal patterns of distress and service utilization with real-world outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic framework for understanding the causal pathways linking place and PTSD outcomes, shifting from correlational hot spot mapping to mechanistic simulation. It will generate testable hypotheses about which specific features of the built and social environment, when interacted with trauma symptoms, most powerfully sustain inequities. The findings will directly inform the development of more effective, spatially targeted public health strategies and resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While Kessler's prior work has excelled in identifying risk factors and testing interventions (e.g., smartphone CBT), this proposal uniquely fuses geospatial hot spot analysis with trauma-informed agent-based modeling to simulate the dynamic, reciprocal causation between individuals and high-risk environments—a causal question that traditional cohort studies cannot directly address.

proposals.title: Computational Phenotyping of Neighborhood Engagement and Stress from Passively Collected Smartphone Data: A Study of Mental Health Risk in Urban Populations
proposals.motivation: Neighborhood environment is a critical social determinant of mental health, but traditional survey-based measures of engagement and stress are episodic and prone to recall bias. The proliferation of smartphones provides a novel, passive, and continuous data stream to quantify real-world behavior and environmental exposures. This study leverages pre-2026 digital phenotyping methods to objectively characterize neighborhood-level factors, addressing a key gap in ecological mental health risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computational phenotypes derived from GPS, accelerometer, and communication logs will reliably quantify patterns of neighborhood engagement (e.g., mobility range, time spent in local amenities) and stress (e.g., circadian disruption, social withdrawal), and that these phenotypes will be significantly associated with concurrent and subsequent symptoms of depression, anxiety, and PTSD.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of 500 adults from an existing urban epidemiological study. Participants' smartphones will collect GPS, accelerometer, call/text metadata, and device usage data for 6 months via a research app (e.g., Beiwe). Using pre-2026 algorithms, we will extract features for mobility (e.g., location variance, routine entropy), activity (e.g., sleep regularity from accelerometry), and social behavior. These features will be clustered via unsupervised learning (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models) to define computational phenotypes. Phenotypes will be validated against ecological momentary assessment (EMA) of stress and neighborhood surveys, then used in regression models to predict mental health outcomes from structured clinical interviews.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable method for measuring neighborhood engagement and stress in real-time, moving beyond static census data. It will establish empirical links between dynamic, behaviorally-derived environmental exposures and mental disorder symptoms, informing targeted public health interventions and personalized risk models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses digital phenotyping for individual-level symptoms, this study innovatively applies it to characterize *neighborhood-level* social processes. It integrates multi-modal sensor data to create composite phenotypes of environmental engagement, a novel construct distinct from simple mobility or social activity metrics.

proposals.title: Modeling the Socio-Spatial Engineering of Neighborhoods: A Longitudinal Study of PTSD Symptom Trajectories as a Driver of Network and Resource Flow
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Kessler's work in the Army STARRS and World Mental Health surveys, has established mental disorders as outcomes shaped by social and environmental contexts. However, the reciprocal pathways—how the behavioral patterns of individuals with different mental health trajectories actively shape their local environments—remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to model the dynamic, bidirectional relationship between population mental health and community-level resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with distinct longitudinal PTSD symptom trajectories (chronic, recovering, resilient) function as 'socio-spatial engineers,' whose patterned behaviors in social interaction, service utilization, and residential mobility predict measurable changes in neighborhood-level social network cohesion and the spatial distribution of economic resources over time.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., Army STARRS-LS, geocoded community surveys) with repeated PTSD assessments (e.g., PCL-5) and linked administrative/geospatial records. Latent class growth mixture modeling will identify PTSD trajectories. For each trajectory class, we will operationalize engineering behaviors via time-varying predictors (e.g., social network size/density from egocentric surveys, frequency of healthcare/community service visits, residential moves). We will then fit multilevel autoregressive and cross-lagged panel models to estimate how these class-specific behaviors predict subsequent neighborhood-level outcomes, such as census-tract aggregates of social fragmentation and economic activity indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for understanding mental health as a dynamic force in community ecology. It will generate empirical estimates of how PTSD trajectories contribute to neighborhood change, moving beyond static risk-factor models. The findings could inform public health interventions aimed at leveraging positive mental health trajectories for community strengthening.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard epidemiological paradigm by treating mental health trajectories as an explanatory variable for environmental change, rather than solely as an outcome. It formally adapts the ecosystem engineer concept from ecology to psychiatric epidemiology, offering a new theoretical lens for person-environment dynamics.

proposals.title: Mapping the Multi-Level Risk Habitat for Post-Traumatic Depression: An Ecological Niche Modeling Approach Integrating Neighborhood Resources and Neural Circuit Markers
proposals.motivation: Understanding why some individuals develop psychiatric disorders like depression after trauma while others do not requires integrating social-environmental and neurobiological risk factors. Pre-2026 research, including the Army STARRS and World Mental Health surveys, highlights the importance of both neighborhood-level resources and individual vulnerabilities, but methods to model their complex, non-linear interactions at scale are lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict risk and identify precise targets for public health and clinical intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical framework of Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) can be successfully adapted to identify and map the multi-level 'risk habitat' for post-traumatic depression, where the disorder's 'distribution' is predicted by critical interactions between external neighborhood resources (the 'environment') and internal neural circuit markers (intrinsic 'trait constraints').
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal data (e.g., from Army STARRS or community cohorts) with geocoded participant locations, trauma exposure, and subsequent depression/substance use outcomes. Neighborhood resource variables (e.g., green space, social capital, healthcare access) will be derived from geospatial databases. A subset will have neural circuit markers (e.g., amygdala reactivity from fMRI, threat sensitivity from behavioral tasks). Using the Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) algorithm—a leading ENM method—we will model the psychiatric outcome as a function of multi-scale environmental and neural variables, treating the latter as intrinsic constraints on the environmental niche. Model performance will be validated using temporal hold-out samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrated predictive model of psychiatric risk following trauma that quantifies the relative contribution and interactions of social-environmental and neural factors. It will generate spatially mappable 'risk landscapes' to inform targeted prevention. The project will also establish a proof-of-concept for adapting computational ecology methods to psychiatric epidemiology, enabling more complex, systems-level modeling of mental health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct methodological translation of Ecological Niche Modeling from conservation biology to mental health research, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy. While multi-level frameworks exist, this approach uniquely uses a species-distribution model to formally treat a psychiatric disorder as an entity with an 'environmental niche' constrained by both external resources and internal biological traits.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Validity Frameworks from Conservation Biology to Trauma Research
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'multi-site, multi-stressor' validation framework from conservation biology to the problem of generalizing trauma research findings. In ecology, to understand if a species' stress response is universal, studies are replicated across diverse habitats and stressor types. Similarly, this research would implement a coordinated, multi-site study treating different trauma populations (e.g., combat veterans, survivors of assault, refugees) and diverse demographic groups (varying in culture, socioeconomic status, geography) as distinct 'ecosystems.' By applying a core set of assessment and intervention protocols across these varied contexts, the study would systematically test for robustness versus context-dependency of findings. The goal is to move beyond simple replication to map the specific 'environmental modifiers'—such as cultural frameworks, social support structures, or trauma characteristics—that explain when and why outcomes generalize or diverge, building a more nuanced, ecological model of trauma response and recovery.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Psychiatric Risk Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling in Conservation Biology to scale the neighborhood-resource-neural framework to depression or substance use after trauma. In ecology, models like MaxEnt integrate multi-scale environmental variables with species occurrence data to predict distribution and identify critical habitat constraints. The adapted research idea applies this computational analogy by treating a psychiatric outcome as the 'species,' neighborhood resources as the 'environmental variables,' and neural circuit markers as intrinsic 'trait constraints.' The result is an integrated predictive model that maps the multi-level 'risk habitat' for the disorder, identifying key interactions between external resources and internal neural vulnerabilities that drive psychiatric risk following trauma.

ideas.title: Adversarial Generalization: Testing Mechanistic Boundaries Across Trauma Populations
ideas.description: This study proactively tests the boundary conditions and mechanistic drivers of the original findings across diverse trauma types and demographic groups. Instead of simple replication, it employs a strategic sampling design that pits pre-registered, competing hypotheses against each other (e.g., does effect attenuation in a new group stem from cultural factors, trauma chronicity, or access to services?). The design incorporates strong counterfactual comparisons and moderation analyses to isolate the specific factors that enable or disrupt the original effect, moving beyond descriptive generalization to a explanatory model of where, why, and for whom the findings hold.

ideas.title: A Within-Family Sibling Comparison Study to Disentangle Gene-Environment Correlation from True Interaction in PTSD Trajectories
ideas.description: This study uses a genetically-informed sibling comparison design to rigorously test for causal gene-environment interaction. It recruits sibling pairs who were raised together but later lived in neighborhoods with divergent resource levels during adolescence or early adulthood. By comparing PTSD trajectories within these pairs and analyzing how the within-pair difference is moderated by their shared polygenic risk or epigenetic markers, the design controls for passive and evocative gene-environment correlation. This approach isolates the true moderating effect of neighborhood context on genetic and epigenetic vulnerability, providing a more robust test of the original hypothesis.

ideas.title: Mechanism-targeted, participatory interventions for neighborhood resources and PTSD
ideas.description: This research co-designs and evaluates neighborhood resource interventions that are explicitly engineered to alter the specific social and psychological mechanisms linking environment to PTSD. Using community-based participatory methods, residents and researchers first identify and design tangible resource improvements (e.g., lighting, green space, community hubs) intended to directly boost perceived safety, social connection, and collective efficacy. The study then implements these interventions in a randomized trial, employing a longitudinal design that measures both changes in the targeted psychosocial mechanisms and PTSD trajectories, rigorously testing the causal pathway from altered resources to improved mental health via social environment change.

ideas.title: Causal Sustenance: Isolating the App's Long-Term Effect from Natural Recovery and Confounders
ideas.description: This study uses a multi-cohort, staggered longitudinal design with an active control group, tracking participants for 12 months post-intervention. To isolate the app's effect, it incorporates frequent ecological momentary assessments (EMA) to capture real-time fluctuations, alongside quarterly detailed surveys that quantify exposure to significant life events and engagement with other mental health or substance use supports. Advanced statistical modeling, including growth mixture modeling and time-varying effect modeling, will be used to distinguish sustained intervention benefits from natural recovery processes and external confounding factors, providing a robust causal estimate of long-term effectiveness.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Models to Track Neural Adaptation to Resource Flux
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological study of succession—tracking how ecosystems change in structure and function in response to resource fluctuations—to the longitudinal tracking of neural and clinical states. Just as ecologists establish permanent plots to monitor species and resources over time to model community transitions, this research would establish a longitudinal human cohort. Participants would undergo periodic fMRI scans to measure neural reward reactivity and clinical assessments for PTSD, while contemporaneous geospatial data would quantify changes in neighborhood resources (e.g., green space, food access, crime rates). The core methodological adaptation involves using ecological state-space or Markov transition models, typically applied to predict successional stages, to instead model the probabilities of transitioning between different neural reactivity-PTSD symptom states as a function of the direction, rate, and type of neighborhood resource change. This provides a dynamic systems framework for understanding how the brain 'successes' or maladapts to its socioeconomic environment over time.

ideas.title: Agent-based simulation of daily navigation within PTSD-resource deprivation hot spots
ideas.description: This research integrates geospatial hot spot analysis and agent-based modeling (ABM) to move beyond identifying static clusters of PTSD risk and resource deprivation. First, hot spot analysis precisely maps these high-risk, low-resource areas. Then, an ABM is built to simulate a synthetic population of agents who must navigate this landscape daily, with behaviors and decision rules informed by trauma-related constraints (e.g., hypervigilance, avoidance). The synergy allows researchers to test how the interaction between the precise spatial structure of deprivation and individual-level navigation strategies dynamically reinforces or mitigates risk, offering causal insights into the formation and persistence of mental health inequities.

ideas.title: Contextual Resource Buffering: Testing a Digital Mental Health App's Differential Efficacy Across Trauma Severity and Cultural Contexts
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the effectiveness of a digital mental health intervention for students is moderated by a three-way interaction between trauma severity, the app's features (as digital resources), and the broader cultural/institutional context of the university. It tests the hypothesis that app-based resources are most beneficial for moderate trauma levels across all settings but become irrelevant for severe trauma in low-support contexts or for mild trauma in high-support contexts. By deploying the app across diverse universities worldwide, the study will determine if the 'resource buffering' model is culturally bound or universal, providing critical design and implementation guidelines for global scalability.

ideas.title: Apply epidemiological cluster detection to PTSD and resource deprivation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodological solution from infectious disease epidemiology—spatial scan statistics—to the context of mental health and resource equity. In epidemiology, tools like SaTScan are used to detect statistically significant spatial clusters of disease, distinguishing true outbreaks from random variation. This methodology can be adapted to identify precise, statistically significant spatial clusters where high PTSD risk and resource deprivation (e.g., lack of mental health providers, socioeconomic disadvantage) co-occur. The adapted approach would involve defining cases (e.g., individuals with PTSD screening positive), a population at risk, and background covariates, then using a spatial scan statistic to detect areas with a significantly higher than expected concentration of cases and resource gaps, providing rigorous, evidence-based targeting for public health intervention.

ideas.title: Hybrid Convergent Primary Outcomes: Mandatory multi-method validation for core constructs
ideas.description: This approach mandates that primary outcomes be defined not by a single measurement method but by the convergent validity of multiple independent methods. For any psychological or behavioral construct, researchers must first decompose it into components best captured by different modalities (subjective experience, observable behavior, physiological correlate). The primary outcome is then operationalized as the agreement or combined evidence from at least two of these distinct measurement streams, such as self-reported symptom severity coupled with a blinded clinician rating or an objective digital behavioral assay. This system overcomes the limitations of purely subjective or purely objective measures by requiring triangulation, ensuring the outcome is both personally meaningful and externally verifiable, thereby increasing scientific rigor and clinical relevance.

ideas.title: Model Gene-Neighborhood Interactions on PTSD Using Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) from ecology to investigate how genetic/epigenetic moderators interact with neighborhood resources to shape PTSD trajectories. In ENM, a species' distribution is predicted by modeling its presence against multiple environmental layers. Here, we treat an individual's genetic/epigenetic risk profile as analogous to a species' intrinsic traits and model PTSD risk or resilience outcomes against multi-dimensional 'neighborhood environment' layers (socioeconomic, physical, social). The adapted solution uses similar machine learning techniques (e.g., MaxEnt) to identify non-linear interactions and thresholds—specific combinations of genetic markers and neighborhood resource levels—that define high-risk or resilient 'niches,' moving beyond simple additive models to discover critical synergistic or buffering effects.

ideas.title: Agent-based modeling of post-trauma recovery pathways across diverse populations and resource landscapes
ideas.description: This research integrates agent-based modeling (ABM) with the study of diverse trauma populations to simulate how individuals with different trauma histories (e.g., interpersonal violence, accidents, disaster exposure) and demographic backgrounds navigate their specific neighborhood resource environments (e.g., clinics, support groups, green spaces) during recovery. The ABM framework allows us to explicitly test hypotheses about how variations in resource distribution, quality, accessibility, and stigma create inequitable recovery trajectories across populations. This moves past simply checking if a finding generalizes, to actively modeling the mechanisms—such as transportation barriers, insurance coverage gaps, or cultural competency of services—that may explain success or failure in different contexts. Ultimately, the simulation can identify high-leverage intervention points in the resource landscape to improve outcomes for specific trauma populations in specific types of neighborhoods.

ideas.title: Ecosystem Engineering: PTSD Symptom Trajectories as a Driver of Socio-Spatial Resource Flow
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'ecosystem engineer' concept from ecology to study the inverse problem of how PTSD trajectories influence neighborhood change. In ecology, engineers like beavers alter landscapes, directing resource flows for entire communities. The analogous solution involves tracking engineer populations and their activities to model environmental modification. Adapted here, individuals with different PTSD symptom trajectories (chronic, recovering, resilient) are conceptualized as socio-spatial engineers whose behavioral patterns—in resource seeking, social bonding, and mobility—actively modify the neighborhood environment. The study would use longitudinal data to model how these trajectory-defined 'engineering' behaviors predict meso-level changes, such as the consolidation or fragmentation of local social networks and the spatial redistribution of economic resources, thereby treating mental health trajectories as a driver of geographic change.

ideas.title: Disentangling Method from Construct: A Multi-Method Test of Subjective vs. Objective Neighborhood Predictors
ideas.description: This study uses a Multi-Trait, Multi-Method (MTMM) design to rigorously compare the predictive power of subjective perceptions and objective metrics of neighborhood safety and support, while controlling for shared method variance. Subjective measures (from surveys) and objective measures (from administrative data, systematic social observation) are separately linked to outcome data collected via entirely different methods, such as administrative health records, behavioral tasks, or physiological biomarkers. This design isolates whether perceived resources offer unique predictive validity beyond objective reality, or if prior findings are artifacts of correlated measurement error. The result is a robust, causally informative test of how different dimensions of neighborhood context influence resident outcomes.

ideas.title: Network-Embedded Substance Use Dynamics: Mapping Social Connectivity to Predict and Explain Polysubstance Use Transitions
ideas.description: This research investigates how the structure and composition of an individual's ego-centric social and resource network within their neighborhood influences transitions in polysubstance use (e.g., alcohol, cannabis, other drugs). By applying network analysis from social sciences, the study will map support connectivity to quantify key metrics like network density, the prevalence of substance-using alters, and access to recovery resources. This network data will then be analyzed alongside longitudinal app-based measures of multiple substance use to test whether specific network configurations predict the diffusion of use from one substance to another or, conversely, foster protective effects. The result is a novel ecological model that explains substance use trajectories as a function of localized network dynamics rather than individual factors alone.

ideas.title: The Placebo of Place: Adapting the Placebo Effect Model to Test if Perceived Neighborhood Quality Drives Health More Than Objective Metrics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core investigative model of the placebo effect from clinical pharmacology to challenge the assumption that objective neighborhood metrics are the primary drivers of resident outcomes. The analogous solution uses controlled trials to separate belief from biochemical reality. The adapted research would conduct a multi-wave study, first establishing baseline objective metrics (e.g., crime stats, income, walkability audits) and resident perceptions. One group then receives a curated 'perceptual intervention'—such as narratives from long-term residents about community support, maps highlighting amenities, or summaries of positive local trends—designed to boost perceived safety and support without altering the objective environment. By comparing this group to a control over time, the study can isolate the causal power of subjective perception on outcomes like psychological well-being, cortisol levels, and social behaviors, testing if perceived resources are a more potent mechanism than objective resources.

ideas.title: National EHR-Linked Trial of Co-Developed vs. Standard Digital Health Tools on Sustained Adolescent Engagement
ideas.description: This study will conduct a large-scale, pragmatic randomized controlled trial across multiple institutions, deploying both a co-developed and an expert-designed digital health application (e.g., for mental wellness or physical activity) to a national cohort of students. By linking participants to their longitudinal electronic health records, the research can move beyond self-reported engagement metrics to objectively assess if higher engagement with the co-developed app correlates with significant changes in real-world health outcomes, such as reduced stress-related clinic visits or improved preventive care adherence. This design directly tests the core hypothesis that co-development leads to more effective tools by measuring their ultimate impact on health, while the scale and heterogeneity of the national cohort allow for examining how socioeconomic, geographic, and institutional factors moderate this effect.

ideas.title: Targeting Individual Resilience Interventions by Neighborhood Resource Stratification
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that the optimal target population for school-based mental health interventions (e.g., for anxiety, ADHD) is not universally the general student body or a clinical subgroup, but is determined by the interaction between individual risk/resilience and neighborhood resource levels. It hypothesizes that in moderately resourced neighborhoods, targeting students with subclinical conditions may yield the greatest impact, as individual resources can be effectively bolstered. However, in profoundly resource-poor neighborhoods, individual-focused interventions may fail without concurrent structural support, suggesting a threshold effect where a general population or community-level approach becomes necessary first to establish a baseline of safety and resources.

ideas.title: Causal Decomposition of Digital Therapeutic Mechanisms via Experimental Factorial Trials
ideas.description: This research employs a full factorial or fractional factorial experimental design to causally test which specific app components (e.g., cognitive restructuring exercises, self-monitoring logs, push notification reminders, social features) drive changes in hypothesized mechanisms (e.g., increased behavioral activation, reduced craving intensity) and subsequent improvements in mental health and substance use outcomes. Participants are randomized to different versions of the app containing systematically varied combinations of active and inactive components, allowing for the isolation of each component's unique and synergistic effects. This method moves beyond correlation to definitively identify the active ingredients and their required dosage, enabling the efficient optimization of future digital interventions.

ideas.title: Causal Test of the 'Resource Paradox': A Natural Experiment on Sudden Resource Influx and Community Recovery Outcomes
ideas.description: This study employs a quasi-experimental, longitudinal design to isolate the causal impact of a sudden increase in neighborhood resources on recovery and well-being. Using a natural experiment framework—such as the unanticipated siting of a major new community center or infrastructure project—it tracks matched treatment and control neighborhoods before and after the resource influx. The research intensively measures not only recovery outcomes (e.g., substance use relapse, mental health, community engagement) but also hypothesized negative mediators like social comparison pressure, isolation, and gentrification pressures. This design directly tests whether the change in resources itself leads to adverse effects, controlling for pre-existing neighborhood characteristics and trends.

ideas.title: Personalized Intervention Dosing: Modeling Optimal Duration and Intensity from Individual Response Trajectories
ideas.description: This research abandons the concept of a fixed, universally optimal intervention period. Instead, it employs a machine learning and adaptive trial design framework to identify how optimal duration and intensity vary across individuals. The system collects rich baseline data and continuously monitors short-term engagement and proximal outcomes during the intervention. It then tests whether algorithms can predict the point of diminishing returns or required exposure for each participant, enabling the design of personalized intervention schedules that maximize efficiency and long-term efficacy.

ideas.title: Targeting the 'Pre-Outbreak' Population: A Public Health Analogy for Student Mental Health
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from public health epidemiology, this idea challenges the original assumption. In disease control, targeting a whole 'pre-outbreak' population with prevention is often more effective than focusing only on the sick. The analogous solution involves community-wide, low-intensity preventative measures to stop subclinical cases from becoming epidemics. Adapted to educational psychology, the research idea proposes that the general student population is the best target for a universal, preventative mental health or academic support intervention. By delivering a low-dose program (e.g., mindfulness, cognitive-behavioral skills) to all students, the study would test if this approach more effectively reduces the overall incidence of severe problems, improves the general climate, and has a greater population-level impact than programs targeting only identified at-risk or diagnosed individuals.

ideas.description: This research applies computational phenotyping to passively collected digital data (e.g., smartphone usage, mobility, communication patterns) from diverse student populations across multiple universities, cultures, and demographic groups. By analyzing this multi-context data, the study aims to identify and validate digital phenotypes of neighborhood engagement and stress that are either universal or specific to particular cultural and institutional settings. This approach allows for a comparative analysis of how the relationship between digital behavior and well-being varies across contexts, providing insights into the environmental and cultural moderators of student mental health. The resulting framework can inform the development of more adaptable and equitable digital health tools tailored to different student life experiences.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Analysis of App Impact on Polysubstance Use via Real-Time Behavioral Mechanisms
ideas.description: This study investigates the causal impact of an alcohol reduction app on cannabis and other drug use by testing specific behavioral mechanisms. It employs a randomized controlled trial with a matched active control, integrating ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to measure daily fluctuations in cravings, self-efficacy, and app use as potential mediators of longer-term substance use outcomes. The analysis uses a formal causal mediation framework to distinguish the app's direct and indirect effects on polysubstance use, moving beyond simple correlation to identify if and how the intervention produces spillover effects.

ideas.title: Dynamic Network-Context Assessment (DyNCA): Real-time mapping of how social and resource network activation shapes mood in urban environments
ideas.description: This research synthesizes ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and social network analysis to investigate how the real-time use and perception of one's social and resource networks within a neighborhood directly influence momentary mood and stress. Smartphone-based EMA prompts will capture an individual's current location, activity, and mood, while simultaneously querying which specific network members (e.g., a neighbor, local shopkeeper) or resources (e.g., a park, community center) are salient, accessible, or being utilized at that moment. By analyzing the temporal sequences of network tie activation alongside contextual experiences, the study can model how the dynamic functionality—not just the structure—of personal networks serves as a micro-mechanism linking neighborhood conditions to psychological well-being.

ideas.title: Federated National Cohort Analysis with Provenance-Aware Harmonization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach applies the analytical framework to a large national cohort of linked EHRs using a federated, multi-site architecture. It first executes a standardized data quality and context assessment at each participating healthcare system to quantify heterogeneity in data capture practices and fitness for specific research questions. The core analysis is then performed only on harmonized data subsets that meet predefined quality thresholds, with all results accompanied by a 'provenance profile' describing the local healthcare contexts. This ensures that national-scale comparisons are robust, interpretable, and explicitly account for the systemic biases inherent in real-world data aggregation.

ideas.title: A Universal Foundation with Embedded Tiered Supports for Student Mental Health
ideas.description: This redesigned approach advocates for delivering core mental well-being and resilience curriculum to the entire student population to maximize preventive impact and destigmatize mental health. This universal Tier 1 foundation actively screens for and identifies students who may benefit from more targeted support, creating a natural funnel into Tier 2 (skill-building groups) and Tier 3 (individual intervention) services. By serving everyone, the system avoids the pitfalls of access barriers tied to diagnosis and reduces stigma, while the embedded tiered structure ensures efficient resource allocation to those with subclinical or diagnosed conditions based on observed need rather than label.

ideas.title: Networked Resilience: Quantifying the Limits of Individual Resource Buffering in Deprived Neighborhoods
ideas.description: This research direction integrates network analysis to map the social and resource ties of resilient individuals within profoundly resource-poor neighborhoods. It tests the hypothesis that individual resources can only buffer neighborhood deficits when an individual's personal network provides sufficient 'bridges' to external resources or critical local support. By quantifying the connectivity, density, and resource flow within these personal networks, the research can identify specific structural network failures—such as isolation or homogenous, resource-poor ties—that even high individual resilience cannot overcome. This provides a mechanistic explanation for the limits of compensation, shifting the focus from individual traits to the interactive system of person and place.

ideas.title: Neural Mechanisms of Peer Coaching: Testing if Human Support Augments Reward Reactivity to Neighborhood Resources
ideas.description: This study investigates whether the presence of a human peer coach is necessary to amplify the brain's reward system reactivity to specific, beneficial neighborhood resources (e.g., community centers, green spaces). We will use a randomized controlled trial design, comparing an app-only group to an app-plus-peer-coaching group, while measuring neural reward reactivity via fMRI in response to cues of identified high-value neighborhood resources. This allows us to disentangle if coaching directly enhances the salience and valuation of these resources in the brain, or if automated guidance alone is sufficient to drive the neural patterns associated with positive behavioral change. The result will identify a potential neurobiological mechanism through which human support translates environmental opportunities into actionable engagement.

ideas.title: National Cohort Analysis of Hyper-Local Resource Effects on Health
ideas.description: This research applies a unified analytical framework to a large national cohort with linked electronic health records to examine how the effects of neighborhood resources on health outcomes vary when measured at the hyper-local block or street segment level versus the census tract level. By scaling the fine-grained geographic analysis to a national population, it can determine if certain block-level effects are universal or context-dependent, revealing interactions between local resources and broader regional characteristics. This approach disentangles the true signal of immediate environmental exposure from the noise of larger administrative units, offering unprecedented precision for policy targeting. The resulting evidence can inform where interventions—like adding a grocery store or green space—would be most impactful across the country.
