ideas.title: Agent-based modeling of paradoxical resource effects on recovery trajectories
ideas.description: This research uses agent-based modeling (ABM) to simulate how individuals with varying psychological profiles and recovery needs perceive, access, and are affected by a high-density neighborhood resource environment. The model uniquely tests mechanisms through which resource abundance can paradoxically hinder recovery, such as by simulating choice overload, social comparison with high-performing peers, or pressure to utilize services, leading to agent isolation or dropout. This allows for virtual experiments to identify specific configurations of resources and community characteristics that trigger these downsides, offering predictive insights for optimizing real-world recovery-supportive environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Measure App Impact on Polysubstance Use
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Community Ecology to extend research on the app's impact. In ecology, introducing a change (like a new predator) requires studying its effect on the entire ecosystem, tracking population changes and interactions among multiple species to identify direct, indirect, and compensatory effects. Adapted to this context, we conceptualize different substances (alcohol, cannabis, other drugs) as species within a user's 'behavioral niche.' The research would measure the app's effect by concurrently tracking changes in the frequency and quantity of all substance use over time. This approach allows us to map the full impact of the intervention, detecting whether reducing alcohol use leads to substitution with other substances, a synergistic reduction across all use, or no effect on other drugs, providing a holistic view of the app's influence on the polysubstance use ecosystem.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Infer Neighborhood Engagement from Digital Footprints
ideas.description: This idea adapts computational niche modeling from ecology to infer neighborhood engagement and stress. In ecology, models like MaxEnt analyze species occurrence points against environmental layers to phenotype habitat preference and stressors. Here, we treat an individual's passively collected digital footprint (location, device usage, communication) as occurrence data. We then model this data against multi-source neighborhood variables (physical, social, and economic layers) to computationally phenotype an individual's unique 'engagement niche'—identifying neighborhood features associated with high engagement or markers of psychosocial stress, all derived from routine digital traces.

ideas.title: Optimizing Intervention Duration via Pharmacokinetic Principles of Dosing Regimens
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a fixed 30-day period is optimal by drawing an analogy from clinical pharmacology. In that field, the optimal drug dosing regimen is determined by pharmacokinetic principles to achieve a therapeutic concentration. The analogous solution involves modeling and testing different schedules (like loading + maintenance doses) to find the best balance of efficacy and tolerability. Adapted to a behavioral intervention context, this becomes a study comparing different 'engagement dosing regimens'—such as a short, intensive 'loading' period versus a longer, steady 'maintenance' schedule—to identify which pattern most effectively and sustainably produces the target psychological or behavioral change, thereby defining optimal intervention dynamics rather than just a fixed duration.

ideas.title: Economic Valuation of Neural Resource Mechanisms for Scalable Digital Intervention Design
ideas.description: This research fuses cost-effectiveness analysis with neuroimaging to determine which specific, scalable digital resources (e.g., virtual social connectivity modules, gamified achievement trackers, on-demand mindfulness content) most efficiently enhance neural reward reactivity in at-risk university students. By applying a neuroeconomic lens, it will quantify the cost per unit of change in key brain reward pathways (e.g., ventral striatum reactivity) elicited by different app-based resource mechanisms. The outcome will be a prioritized, cost-optimized blueprint for scaling the Minder app, where investment is directed specifically toward the digital features proven to deliver the greatest neurobiological benefit per dollar, thereby maximizing the intervention's real-world impact and economic viability for university populations.

ideas.title: The Resource Toxicity Threshold: Applying Ecological Carrying Capacity Models to Neighborhood Recovery
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological principle of carrying capacity and resource toxicity to investigate the downsides of high neighborhood resources on recovery. Just as an overabundance of nutrients can lead to eutrophication and ecosystem collapse in ecology, an over-concentration of recovery-oriented resources (e.g., treatment centers, support groups, sober living homes) may create unintended negative effects like performance pressure, stigma saturation, or social isolation for residents. The study will employ methods from ecosystem modeling to identify optimal resource thresholds and curvilinear relationships, aiming to develop guidelines for balancing neighborhood resource density to maximize recovery support without triggering these paradoxical detrimental effects.

ideas.title: Contextual Mechanism Mapping for Cross-Population App Evaluation
ideas.description: This research system first identifies the specific cultural, institutional, and socio-economic mechanisms hypothesized to influence the educational app's effectiveness within diverse student populations. Using a mixed-methods, sequential design, it engages local stakeholders at each site to map these contextual factors before launching a coordinated multi-site trial. The subsequent quantitative evaluation is explicitly designed to test the pre-registered hypotheses about these mechanisms, explaining why effectiveness varies rather than just documenting that it does. This approach ensures findings are interpretable, actionable for localization, and robust against the pitfalls of superficial diversity sampling.

ideas.title: Identifying the Minimal Sufficient System: Isolating the Active Ingredients of Support
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple sufficiency test to isolate the active components of effective support. It employs a sequential, multi-architecture experimental design that iteratively tests and refines distinct automated intervention systems against a benchmark of human peer coaching. The goal is not to prove a binary necessity but to determine if a well-designed automated system can achieve comparable outcomes, and if not, to precisely characterize the functional elements (e.g., accountability, empathy, adaptability) that remain uniquely human. This yields a generative map of the design space for effective support systems rather than a simple yes/no answer.

ideas.title: National Health Cohort Analysis Using Distributed Computing Architecture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the distributed computing solution from large-scale astronomy surveys (like the Sloan Digital Sky Survey) to the challenge of scaling health research to national cohorts. In astronomy, petabytes of data from multiple telescopes are processed using a federated model where data is standardized but remains locally, with distributed query systems enabling large-scale analysis without centralization. Adapted to health research, this means creating a secure technical and governance framework where linked electronic health records from a national cohort remain within their local or regional health information networks or trusted research environments. A central coordinating body would distribute standardized analytical code and queries to these nodes. Each node processes the query against its local data, returning only aggregated, de-identified summary statistics or model parameters. These results are then combined to generate national-scale insights, enabling cohort comparisons while maintaining patient privacy, complying with data residency regulations, and overcoming the technical bottleneck of moving or centralizing massive, sensitive datasets.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Hierarchical Model to Resolve Spatial Aggregation Bias in Local Resource Effects
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simply choosing a single hyper-local unit by developing a Bayesian hierarchical model that integrates data at multiple spatial scales—from individual addresses to census tracts—within a single analytical framework. The model estimates how the measured effect of a resource (e.g., a park, grocery store) on an outcome (e.g., health, property value) varies across these scales and identifies the most relevant scale of influence for each resource-outcome pair. This approach directly quantifies the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem, providing robust, scale-aware effect estimates that are not artifacts of arbitrary administrative boundaries. The result is a more generalizable and methodologically sound understanding of how neighborhood resources operate across geography.

ideas.title: Network-Responsive Peer Coaching: Optimizing Human Support Placement via Social Graph Analysis
ideas.description: This research tests whether the necessity of human peer coaches in a digital intervention depends on an individual's position within their local social and resource network. It maps participants' support connectivity using transfer network analysis from the social sciences to quantify metrics like isolation, centrality, and resource access. The core experiment then randomizes peer coaching support not simply on/off, but strategically targets it to individuals based on their network profiles (e.g., isolated nodes vs. well-connected hubs). This tests the hypothesis that automated app components may be sufficient for well-connected individuals, while human coaching is critical for those in fragmented or resource-poor network positions to drive both individual outcomes and network-level diffusion of benefits.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Forecast the Cost-Effectiveness of Scaling a Digital Health Intervention
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling to assess the cost-effectiveness of scaling the Minder app. In conservation biology, species distribution models forecast where a species can successfully establish by matching its niche requirements to new environments. The analogous solution is adapted by defining the 'intervention niche' of the Minder app—the specific combination of university-level variables (e.g., existing mental health infrastructure, student demographics, administrative support, budget) correlated with its historical cost-effectiveness. By building a predictive model based on data from initial pilot universities, the research would map this niche onto potential scaling targets. This allows for a forecast of the economic costs, benefits, and likelihood of successful, cost-effective implementation at new universities, prioritizing expansion to the most suitable 'niches' and providing a data-driven scaling roadmap.

ideas.title: Quantifying Neighborhood Support Resilience via Ecological Food Web Stability Metrics
ideas.description: This idea adapts network stability analysis from ecology to the social science context of neighborhood support mapping. In ecology, food web models quantify an ecosystem's resilience by analyzing the network structure of who eats whom, using metrics like connectance and modularity. By mapping individuals' social and resource networks within a neighborhood, we can treat these support exchanges as analogous to trophic links. Applying established ecological network analysis tools allows us to quantify the support system's connectivity and, crucially, its structural robustness—predicting how the loss of key individuals or resources might cascade through the community, thereby identifying 'keystone' supporters and vulnerabilities in the social fabric.

ideas.title: Triangulated Behavioral and Digital Network Analysis for Empirical Support Flow Mapping
ideas.description: This research system maps neighborhood support connectivity by converging three distinct data streams to overcome measurement bias. It integrates surveyed perceived networks with analyzed digital interaction traces from local platforms and, crucially, embeds structured behavioral experiments that reveal actual resource-sharing preferences among participants. This triangulation allows the quantification of not just the structure but the strength and reliability of support ties, differentiating latent, active, and nominal connections. The result is a robust, empirically weighted support network map that validates connectivity against observable behavior.

ideas.title: Testing the Limits of Compensation: Does Private Vehicle Access Buffer the Economic Penalty of Neighborhood Job Sprawl?
ideas.description: This research precisely tests whether a key individual resource—reliable private vehicle access—can buffer residents of neighborhoods with poor job proximity from negative economic outcomes. Using longitudinal data and propensity score matching, it compares the employment and income trajectories of high-resource individuals (vehicle access, higher education) living in high job-sprawl neighborhoods against statistically similar peers in low job-sprawl areas. The design establishes a clear, falsifiable test: if the high-resource group in poor neighborhoods shows no significant economic penalty, it supports buffering; a persistent penalty reveals a hard limit to individual compensation, pinpointing where neighborhood deficits overwhelm personal resources.

ideas.title: Testing the 'Participatory Design' Assumption in Educational Apps via a Community Ecology Framework
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that co-development guarantees higher long-term student engagement by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, the 'Field of Dreams' hypothesis—that creating a habitat ensures a thriving ecosystem—is tested by comparing active restoration (species introduction and management) against passive restoration (habitat creation alone). Adapting this, the study would treat educational apps as 'digital habitats' and student engagement as the 'ecosystem health.' It would conduct a controlled, longitudinal comparison between apps co-developed with students (active restoration) and those designed solely by experts (passive restoration). The adapted solution involves tracking ecological analog metrics over 6-12 months: user retention rates (species persistence), the variety and depth of feature usage (biodiversity), and the emergence of peer support networks or sustained community interaction (ecosystem stability and network resilience). This framework moves beyond simple satisfaction surveys to test whether co-development genuinely fosters a self-sustaining 'ecosystem' of engagement or if expert design can achieve similar or better long-term outcomes.

ideas.title: Testing the 'Human-in-the-Loop' Necessity in Digital Behavioral Interventions
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that an app's automated components are sufficient for effectiveness by testing the necessity of human peer coaching support. Drawing an analogy from Conservation Ecology, where purely technological restoration systems (e.g., drone seeding) often fail without the adaptive, context-sensitive role of human community stewards, we adapt this 'human-in-the-loop' solution. The adapted study will empirically compare outcomes across three conditions: a fully automated app, an app augmented with simulated peer support (to control for the mere presence of communication), and an app integrated with real human peer coaches. The aim is to isolate and identify the unique, non-automatable functions human coaches provide—such as genuine empathy, dynamic motivational responding, and complex problem-solving—that may be critical for sustained behavioral change, thereby testing whether a hybrid human-technology system is necessary for optimal effectiveness.

ideas.title: Micro-Environmental Resource Gradients: A Landscape Ecology Approach to Urban Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a core methodology from Landscape Ecology to urban studies. In ecology, processes are understood by examining fine-scale patches and the gradients between them, rather than large, homogeneous zones. The analogous solution involves using high-resolution data to define functional units and measure within-unit variation. Adapted to the original idea, the research will examine resource effects by scaling down to the hyper-local block or street segment level. It will treat these as distinct 'urban patches' and use fine-grain data sources to map and analyze resource gradients and edge effects within and between these patches, providing a more precise and mechanistically informative picture than census tract-level analysis can offer.

ideas.title: Participatory, data-grounded agent-based modeling of neighborhood resource navigation
ideas.description: This research employs a two-stage, mixed-methods approach to create a robust agent-based model of neighborhood resource access. First, agent rules and decision-making heuristics are co-developed with community residents using participatory workshops and mobile ethnography, capturing nuanced local knowledge and constraints. Second, the simulation is calibrated and validated against high-resolution behavioral datasets, such as anonymized GPS traces or transit card data, to ensure it replicates observed mobility and access patterns. This integration of qualitative, ground-truth social processes with quantitative behavioral data produces a policy-relevant model that accurately reflects the complex, often inequitable realities of navigating urban resources.
